responseSet.title: Introduction: Concluding the JAPA Series on Winnicott's Collected Works
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: Concluding the JAPA Series on Winnicott's Collected Works

Phillip Bromberg and Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

Email: pb6638@gmail.com

Adrienne Harris  

It is fitting that Lesley Caldwell has written this final entry in our eleven-essay review of The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott. As editor of the Collected Works, along with Helen Taylor Robinson, she is wonderfully situated to recount the history of how this monumental project came to fruition and the circuitous path it took to reach completion.

Caldwell witnessed the evolution of Winnicott's international reputation and notes how his work took on different saliences in various analytic cultures. In a sense, there are multiple Winnicotts—each framed by the controversies and politics of the society in which the work was encountered. Caldwell speculates how different these JAPA review essays might have been had they been written by British analysts who, like Winnicott, lived through the tempestuous intellectual environment of the British Psychoanalytical Society. It is a measure of Winnicott's greatness that he touches on so many of the issues that have consumed analytic thinkers regardless of their profoundly different analytic cultures. And our reviewers have captured, as well, his vast reach beyond the consulting room, from pediatricians and teachers to parents and the greater public.

This review project began in mid-2019 and took almost five years to complete. None of this would have been possible without the support, generosity, and wisdom of JAPA editor-in-chief Mitchell Wilson and the superb book review editors Anne Adelman and Jennifer Stuart. We owe them immense gratitude.

Email: pb6638@gmail.com

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obsessions and Compulsions: A Lifespan Perspective

Gregory Rizzolo

Gregory Rizzolo

122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1450
Chicago, IL 60603
Email: greg.rizzolo@depthcounseling.org
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2897-3931

Gregory Rizzolo  

identification, OCD, OCPD, lifespan development, obsessive-compulsive disorder, ideal self, narrative identity, introjection, behavior therapy, exposure and response prevention (ERP)

Freud traced the origin of the obsessional neurosis, which he considered a model condition for psychoanalytic inquiry, to a fixation in the anal phase of psychosexual development. Although many analysts have raised doubts about his account, and while the Sullivanian and Lacanian traditions have proposed alternatives, no approach has accounted for what Freud observed as the dizzying variety of obsessive presentations, which seem to defy a singular explanation. The broader research community has moved on, meanwhile, to genetic, neurological, and cognitive-behavioral explanations of what we now call obsessive-compulsive disorder. I argue that we can best account for the variety of obsessive presentations and meaningfully contribute to this interdisciplinary dialogue by framing obsessive-compulsive symptoms as the result of a disorder of volition, an exaggerated sense of willpower, not tied to any one developmental phase or bodily zone. Such a disorder evolves through the lifespan processes of introjection, identification, and repudiation in relation to an anxious/critical parent or an unpredictable environment. I trace these processes through three major developmental milestones. The implication is that, by looking in depth at how the obsessive person internalizes relationships, psychoanalysis can make a unique contribution to a conversation beyond its own borders.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Uncanny Teletherapy: Working With Extimacy

Benjamin Strosberg , Derek Hook and Sean Leadem

Sean Leadem

211 Rockwell Hall
600 Forbes Avenue
Pittsburgh, PA 15218
Email: strosbergb@duq.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2085-775X

Sean Leadem  

teletherapy, psychotherapy, psychoanalysis, Covid-19, uncanny, intimacy, extimacy, Lacan

Responses to the question of teletherapy have multiplied over the past decades, yet many therapists are grappling with the challenges and opportunities of teletherapy for the first time in the wake of the Covid-19 pandemic. For many clinicians, teletherapy was, at least initially, unfamiliar and unsettling. Much of the literature construes teletherapy as a degradation of an original—incapable of providing the intimacy achieved and maintained in two-bodies-in-a-room therapy—or as a good enough simulation. Both positions risk enshrining the consulting room as offering an unmediated scene of therapy. An alternative approach to the strangeness, distance, and mediations so prominent in the transition to teletherapy takes up a qualified sense of Freud's concept of the uncanny: the “minimal uncanny” and its reformulation in Lacanian theory as extimacy, a lens through which to explore intimacy and bodily proximity in the transition to teletherapy. Through the logic of extimacy, a theoretical view emerges that approaches teletherapy not as a poor substitute for real therapy, or a good enough substitute, but as an exemplary case of the therapeutic encounter.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Autotheory: Toward the Embodying of Analytic Framing

Daria Colombo

Daria Colombo

35 East 85th Street
New York, NY 10028
Email: dcolombo8@gmail.com

Daria Colombo  

autotheory, feminist theory, interdisciplinary, psychoanalytic framing

Autotheory is an emerging idea in feminist theory that emphasizes the inevitability of the subjective and embodied personal within any development, understanding, or application of theory. Autotheory offers a way to bring the sexed and gendered body of the analyst back into view in clinical practice and is a possible route to relibidinizing psychoanalytic theory. The embodied part of the “personal” contribution of the analyst will be examined through conceptions of framing and the frame, extending and further conceptualizing ideas prominently addressed within relational and intersubjective theories. Framing activity as a developmental goal for psychoanalytic process is considered in relation to the ways in which autotheoretical texts enact or depict the unceasing interplay of relational experience and theory that forms subjectivity. A clinical vignette describes the experience of framing activity during remote videoconference therapy.

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Most Hysterical Psychoanalyst

Jamieson Webster

Jamieson Webster

65 Fifth Avenue
New York, NY 10011-8603
Email: jamieson.websterphd@gmail.com

Jamieson Webster  

hysteria, Lacan, Freud, Dora, language, desire

This paper is an investigation into the prominence of hysteria in Lacan's work and the enduring significance of the diagnosis for contemporary practice. Beginning with Lacan's theory of neurosis, the importance of language, and symbolic functions, we will begin to understand why the hysterical symptom is the symptomatic structure par excellence. Lacan lauds hysteria as the neurosis in direct dialogue with a given historical moment, teaching the psychoanalyst where we are in the unfolding struggle between neurosis and civilization. He returned to the case of Dora throughout his twenty-eight years of teaching to refine his work. He even saw psychoanalysis as the progressive “hystericization” of the patient and depicted himself as an hysteric walking around on a stage, not knowing what he was saying, while attempting to teach the psychoanalysts. Lacan's reading of Dora seems to mark important shifts in his own life, from his beginning to write as a psychoanalyst, to being thrown out of the IPA, to struggling with his own school and the ensuing political eruptions in France in 1968. By tracking his elaboration of Dora we can witness the evolution of Lacan's work and how he uniquely positions the analyst in the transference.

In Dreams Begin Responsibilities: Facing Ethical Fragility in Psychoanalysis

Jane Kite

Jane Kite

209 Pearl Street
Cambridge, MA 02139
Email: jkite@gmail.com

Jane Kite  

It may be that, as Bergson maintained, a philosopher only ever develops one idea. In any case, there is no doubt that the philosopher is born of a single question, the question which arises at the intersection of thought and life at a given moment in the philosopher's youth; the question which one must at all costs find a way to answer.

— Alain Badiou (2012, p. vi)

That psychoanalysis is deep down a practice founded on ethics may seem obvious, even trivial, and, for that reason, be seriously underestimated.

— Dominique Scarfone (2017, p. 394)

First Dream

The year is 1976, and my mother is long dead. Having just received my doctorate in clinical psychology, I dream that night that I am standing at the bottom of a long escalator, confidently holding a briefcase that looks suspiciously like an old-fashioned doctor's bag. Looking up, I see my mother slowly descending, each metal stair folding neatly into the next, bringing her closer. She looks characteristically anxious, not quite all there. When she gets to the bottom and we are face to face I say to her confidently, hopefully: “Mommy, I'm a doctor now, and I'm here to take care of you.”

My responsibilities as a newly minted psychologist in this dream are clear. The dream didn't surprise me. In fact, it presented a picture that was already known to me. But I still needed to know why. How had things

[[RunningHead]]

Comings and Goings

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Email: mdwilsonmd@berkeley.edu

Mitchell Wilson  

JAPA benefits greatly from the careful and dedicated work of its Associate Editors, who write many of the decision letters to authors and shepherd papers through the review process. This past year Katie Aafjes-van Doorn, Sarah Ackerman, Phillip Blumberg, Anne Erreich, Jane Kite, Stephen Portuges, Greg Rizzolo, and Lynne Zeavin continued their outstanding work as Associate Editors. Anne Adelman and Jennifer Stuart are co-editors of the JAPA Review of Books, with Rachel Boué Widawsky as editor for foreign books. We thank all of them for their efforts on behalf of JAPA.

We say goodbye to the following Editorial Board members, who made significant contributions to the journal during the past three years and who rotated off the Board on December 31, 2022: Rodrigo Barahona, Alison Brown, Michael Garfinkle, Karen Gilmore, Chris Lovett, Elizabeth Lunbeck, Kevin Meehan, Joyce Slochower, and Kathryn Zerbe. The following colleagues have been appointed to the Editorial Board for the term January 1, 2023, through December 31, 2025: Ricardo Ainslie, Andrea Celenza, Lena Ehrlich, Charles Fisher, Henry Freidman, Lee Grossman, Brian Kloppenberg, and Henry Markman. Wes Alwan, Luis Ripoli, Tim Sawyier, and Hannah Zeavin continue as Editorial Associates for another year. They are joined by Jyoti Rao and Daniel Butler for two-year terms.

Mitchell Wilson

Email: mdwilsonmd@berkeley.edu

responseSet.title: From the Enigma of Identity to “Becoming a Subject”: The Transitional Double
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From the Enigma of Identity to “Becoming a Subject”: The Transitional Double

Johann Jung

Johann Jung

5, Avenue Pierre Mendes France
Bron 69676
FRANCE
Email: johannjung@hotmail.fr
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1413-6772

Johann Jung  

transitional double, identity, subjectivity, reflexivity, double, intersubjectivity, primary narcissism, otherness, psychic mirror

In the field of psychopathology, “narcissistic and identity-related suffering” refers to a type of suffering characterized by a lack of being that centrally affects narcissism and identity continuity/discontinuity. Present in many clinical and psychopathological pictures, these problems in turn invite us to undertake a rereading of the modalities of structuration of subjectivity in the course of development. Elements for a model of identity construction are proposed based on the paradigm of the double. Approached from the angle of paradox, identity is thought of as a process in the service of “becoming a subject” based, essentially, on the role of the object and its reflexive function. Drawing on the concept of “transitional double,” this perspective allows the foundations of subjective identity and their stages of construction to be described; these foundations underlie the creation of an internal psychic mirror, the locus of one's relationship to self. These considerations give us a better understanding of the logics of narcissistic and identity-related pathologies, which are characterized in particular by a failure of reflexive capacities, revealing the uncertainties of the dual relational dynamic in the course of early development.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Psychoanalytic Institute's Response to Existential Threats: A Case Study of Organizational Self-Inquiry and External Consultation

James Barron , Richard Honig and Phil Lebovitz

Phil Lebovitz

233 Harvard Street, Suite 207
Brookline, MA 02445
Email: jwbarron101@gmail.com

Phil Lebovitz  

institutes, case study, existential threat, organizational consultation, organizational dynamics, organizational diagnosis, organizational resilience, social defense, leadership development, intergenerational conflict

The term institute is used inclusively here to refer to different organizational structures such as psychoanalytic societies and centers. Those organizations have primary tasks such as providing education and training in psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic psychotherapy. Existential threats covers a range of factors, both internal and external to an organization, that may seriously impair or destroy its capacity to carry out its primary tasks and to survive as a functioning entity. Perceptions and responses relating to those threats are dynamic processes within the organization that shift and evolve over time. This case study explores one institute's use of organizational self-inquiry and external consultation to strengthen its capacity to perceive, make meaning of, and respond adaptively to those threats. The qualitative research for this case study is based on a series of semistructured individual interviews with a representative sample of participants in the consultation, close attention to the intersubjective experiences of interviewees and interviewers, and careful thematic analysis of the interview data. Interviewees shared their understanding of what led up to the consultation, their experience of the consultation, and their perception of its immediate and ongoing impact. Many interviewees felt that the consultation helped strengthen the institute's organizational capacity for resilience and innovation, expressed the desire for additional consultation to ensure the institute's survival and ongoing health, recommended that the institute include the study of organizational dynamics in its curriculum, and thought it should develop its internal capacity for organizational self-inquiry.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In The Session: Introduction

Lynne Zeavin

Lynne Zeavin

80 University Place, Fifth Floor
New York, NY 10003
Email: drlynne.zeavin@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3427-6710

Lynne Zeavin  

We are pleased to announce a new section of the journal that we are calling “In the Session.” In the Session will feature a psychoanalyst's description of a brief clinical moment that is remarkable for its striking, confusing, pleasing, upsetting, surprising, or disruptive features. The analyst in such situations can feel perplexed or anxious, perhaps temporarily stuck. Such moments often call for the analyst to pivot to another conceptual or technical approach, or to spontaneously do or say something out of the ordinary-in ways not anticipated. These are the moments we are interested in thinking about.

We will ask an analyst to depict such a moment-likely familiar to all psychoanalytic clinicians in one way or another-as transparently as possible. We will then ask two other analysts to write brief commentaries on that account.

For our inaugural In the Session, Henry Markman vividly depicts a vexing psychoanalytic situation that is both technically complex and emotionally demanding, for patient and analyst alike. To reach his patient, Markman has to search his own experience of disruption; this allows him to generate a novel way of working and relating to his patient, Matt. Donnel Stern and Shelley Rockwell offer sensitive and thoughtful commentaries from within their own theoretical frameworks, interpersonal and Kleinian, respectively.

The manner in which any one of us enters into the experience of another clinician says a great deal about how we consider all that is happening in the privacy of the analytic relationship. As Markman notes, the clinician presenting work wants first to be understood from inside the situation. This requires that the reader/listener be as attentive as possible to the complexity and strain of analytic work, taking neither a too-knowing view nor a too observational, distant perspective.

Our hope is that readers will put themselves as fully as possible into the material presented here in ways that can be resonant, evocative, and enlivening.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A JAPA Vignette: A Moment with Matt

Henry Markman

Henry Markman

2435 Henry Russell Street
San Francisco, CA 94143-0410
Email: hcmarkman@gmail.com

Henry Markman  

Matt and I were not communicating. I was often unclear what he meant. If asked for clarification, he would reply, “I don't know, I don't know.” My way of speaking confused him. I noticed his long pauses after I spoke. He might then say, “I don't know what to say,” or “Why did you say that? I don't know what you mean.” He told me the problem was that I jumped around and made links he could not follow, including connecting what he said to our relationship. The words I used for his emotions did not resonate. We were cut off from each other.

Several times Matt suddenly entered terrified states, curling up in a ball, saying, “I don't know, I don't know what is real!” sobbing and hyperventilating. This was usually preceded by an agitated state that escalated the more I tried to understand and help. I felt especially off balance, disoriented, and cloudy-headed. When I tried to put words to his experience, he would say, “I know that already,” or “That doesn't help.” If I could gather myself up during his panic, I might try to help him by simply calling his name several times, telling him I am here with him, suggesting he breathe slowly with me. I was not sure if this helped, though he seemed to listen, and over several minutes he gradually calmed down and remained silent for the rest of the session.

These episodes deeply disturbed me: the intensity of his out-ofcontrol suffering and my terrible feeling of uselessness. It bordered on something traumatic for me as well. I didn't know why I was so thrown off and where this was coming from in me. These emotional states are something I've faced before many times, as most analysts have. I understand

Training and Supervising Analyst and faculty, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis.

Submitted for publication September 24, 2022; accepted September 29, 2022.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Markman's Vignette

Donnel Stern

Donnel Stern

20 West 74th Street
New York, NY 10023
Email: donnelstern@gmail.com

Donnel Stern  

The mere fact that JAPA has presented Henry Markman's vignette for discussion represents for me a bit of encouragement about the future of our field. I am encouraged not simply because I applaud what Markman did (although I do feel that way about it, under the circumstances in which it came about). No, what I appreciate is Markman's cautious openness to following his patient's emphatic lead, without feeling that he was disastrously violating his professional ideals. Markman trusted the patient to know something about what he needed. He listened, and he considered the alternatives available to him, and he chose selfdisclosure, or what I would prefer to call expressive participation.

Markman doesn't tell us the theoretical position from which he is working, although we are clear that he does not agree with the most traditional views, at least in this instance. We are safe in assuming, I think, that Markman is working from some kind of field theory, in which the events of the treatment are mutually constructed by the participants. The processes by which the field is constructed, and often enough the resulting outcomes, too, are liable to occur outside awareness, and do not necessarily lend themselves to representation in any kind of symbolic terms-that is, neither in imagery of some kind nor in words. Instead, at least in North American field theory,1 moments of clinical process such as the one we are discussing between Matt and Markman-and human relatedness of all kinds, for that matter-may not be represented in the mind by symbols 1The phrase “North American field theory” is used to differentiate this kind of field theory from the post-Bionian variety. I cannot address their similarities and differences here, but see Stern 2013, 2020.Training and Supervising Analyst, William Alanson White Institute; Adjunct Assistant Professor of Psychology, NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.Submitted for publication December 1, 2022; accepted December 3, 2022.

[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on Markman's Vignette

Shelley Rockwell

Shelley Rockwell

3303 Macomb Street NW
Washington, DC 20008
Email: srockwell33@aol.com

Shelley Rockwell  

Patients are … anxious about the analyst's capacity to understand them… . Children do research on the analyst's capacity for reverie and bring material for the purpose of testing whether he can think, notice [and] remember … emotionally understand-as opposed to verbally mechanically, or from books… . Can he know me? And how does he do it? Anxiety-ridden research into the analyst's mind rather than into his own may be the focus of the analysis for a long time.

—Edna O'Shaugnessy (1981, p. 90)

Henry Markman has provided us a provocative and moving account of a difficult clinical situation, one that would befuddle any of us, possibly driving one into helplessness and despair. Raising many questions, Markman presents his dilemma in a manner inviting us to think, and to consider his work in an open manner.

I will begin my discussion with Markman's bold opening statement, “Matt and I were not communicating.” He continues, describing an impasse, including numerous instances of “not knowing” going both directions between patient and analyst. Bion taught us that “not knowing” is a crucial state of mind to be heeded by the analyst. It is its own state, to be taken on its own terms and, one hopes, eventually “understood,” but first felt to be true and important. Melanie Klein wrote movingly about the young child's urgent desire to know his or her mother, both mind and body-the epistemophilic drive, thereby imputing a third instinct to the human mind. Bion (1961) went on to develop these ideas in his groundbreaking theory of thinking.

Training and Supervising Analyst, the Contemporary Freudian Society and the Washington Baltimore Center for Psychoanalysis.

Submitted for publication November 24, 2022; accepted December 3, 2022.

119

[[RunningHead]]

From the Editors

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3372-3750

Email: jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-6104-8978

Email: rsboue@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2403-0549

Rachel Widawsky  

In this first issue of 2023, we offer a sampling of psychoanalytic authors’ efforts to illuminate—in ways both artistic and scholarly—matters of interest to us as clinicians, as lifelong students of psychoanalysis, and as citizens of the contemporary world. We begin with two book essays, each on two psychoanalyst-authors’ ventures into creative territory: a novel for young adults, a memoir, and two novels for adult readers.

Maternal Loss in Adolescence

An avid reader of psychoanalysts’ forays into creative territory, Sybil Houlding views one experience—a teenage girl's loss of her mother— through two lenses: Kerry Malawista's young adult novel Meet the Moon and Irene Hoge Smith's memoir The Good Poetic Mother. Houlding characterizes as “good-enough” the mother that Malawista's fictional protagonist—Jody, a young teen—loses to death in a shocking car crash. Jody has a good deal of support from surviving family and from the community at large. By contrast, Hoge Smith's real-life mother abandoned her family when the memoirist was a young teen, fleeing a “bullying and neglectful” husband for another man, a prominent poet (whose inscription in a book gifted to Hoge Smith's mother provides the memoir its title). Unlike Malawista's fictional character, Hoge Smith was largely on her own to navigate adolescence after her mother abandoned the family. Reflecting on any clinician's role in helping a teen through maternal loss, Houlding writes of these two books that they “remind us … where maternal loss is part of the history … each person is unique, each outcome is uncertain.”

Seeing the World: Now and Then, Without and Within

Barbara Stimmel contributes a thoughtful essay on two novels written by psychoanalysts: The Distance from Home, by Dan Jacobs, and Memory's Eyes: A New York Oedipus Novel, by Cordelia SchmidtHellerau. Stimmel invites us to consider what is distinct about novels 125

[[RunningHead]]

Maternal Loss in Adolescence

Meet the Moon. By Kerry Leddy Malawista. Raleigh, NC: Fitzroy Books, 2022, 254 pp., $18.95 paperback.The Good Poetic Mother: A Daughter's Memoir. By Irene Hoge Smith. New York: IPBooks, 2021, xvi + 232 pp., $28.98 paperback.

Sybil Houlding

Sybil Houlding

303 Whitney Avenue
New Haven, CT 06510
Email: sybil.houlding@gmail.com

Sybil Houlding   Dean of Faculty, Western New England Institute for Psychoanalysis.

In the first draft of their lives together, there had always been a push and pull between them, a closeness and a desire to be rid of that closeness, neither of them being able to fix their uncomfortable nearness and farness, or to understand what distance was best. Then they were one body [Heti 2022, p. 76].

There is much more continuity between intra-uterine life than the impressive caesura of birth would have us believe. What happens is that the child's biological situation as a foetus is replaced for it by a psychical object-relation to its mother [Freud 1926, p. 168].

So begins the lifelong process of separation-individuation. This transition from inside to outside, which begins for the mother before her baby emerges into the world, is the foundation of all that will follow in the long journey toward emancipation—one that in adolescence evokes special challenges for both parties, and replicates, in some measure, the early preverbal period.

In Inside Lives, Margot Waddell (2002) writes, “Puberty is a time when bodily changes occur more rapidly than any other period in life, except in the womb” (p. 139). The two periods of development are linked by rapid growth and separation from a primary object. A good-enough mother, in Winnicott's iconic phrase, is essential in the neonatal period, and her enduring presence will both challenge and support the child's entrance into adolescence. I will include one more quote, this time from Loewald (1962), to underline this tension.

131

But we shall have to follow a roundabout path in order to explain how science sets about restoring to words a part at least of their former magical power.

—Freud (1890, p. 283)

An author's words, after all, are deeds.

—Freud, letter to Thomas Mann, 1935

The writer ’s joy is the thought that can become an emotion, the emotion that can wholly become a thought.

—Thomas Mann, Death in Venice (1912(

In the first of my epigraphs, Freud invites the reader to follow him as he unfolds the secrets of psychoanalytic technique or, as it soon enough came to be known, “the talking cure.” The magic of words and their impact on self-knowledge, self-suasion, and self-recovery has proved a steadfast component of most forms of psychotherapy, regardless of provenance. In the second, Freud tells Thomas Mann, a writer he greatly admires, that in writing a novel he has created an honest deed. Finally, Mann, although not responding directly to Freud, captures an essential truth about the psychoanalyst's joy as well.

I chose these three epigraphs because, as I hope to show, they bring us close to the subject of this essay, novels by Daniel Jacobs and Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau. Looking at the books first separately, then together, I hope to convey their pleasures and points of invitation to wander afar, as we hope to do when reading a novel. I will be careful not to reveal too much of their plot lines or major character developments, instead 141

In The Hidden Spring: A Journey to the Source of Consciousness, Mark Solms demonstrates the value of neuropsychoanalytic ideas in furthering the psychoanalytic enterprise. His work is valuable for both the novice and the experienced clinician. In this volume he explores several themes: (1) feelings as indicators of the presence or absence of homeostasis, (2) the importance of understanding the concept of “free energy” as described by Karl Friston; and (3) the nature of consciousness, considered to be the “hard problem” of neuroscience. Dating back to Freud's ideas, many neuroscientists, including contemporary figures like Joseph LeDoux, have considered consciousness to be a function of the cerebral cortex. However, Solms, the late Jaak Panksepp, Antonio Damasio, and Bjorn Merker consider the origin of consciousness to lie in the subcortical region.

For Solms, any state of feeling is exactly equal to experiencing the state of consciousness. One is always conscious when one feels (good or bad and everything in between). And so, to feel is to be conscious. He expands on some of the universally shared human feeling states (pain, fear, panic, sorrow), which come from the part of the brain that we share with other vertebrates. Hence his title, The Hidden Spring. Solms demonstrates that cognition is mainly unconscious, whereas consciousness is engineerable, or, as Friston, a computational neuroscientist, has described it: a problem of the physics of brain activity. For Solms the “hard problem” of consciousness is easier to solve by considering that feelings lead to consciousness, that affects arise from subcortical areas as in other mammals, and that, as a result of the new field of computational neuroscience, consciousness is artificially producible.

There are twelve chapters in the book, plus an introduction and a postscript. The chapter topics include dreams; before and after Freud; the cortical fallacy; what is experienced; feelings; the source; the free energy principle; a predictive hierarchy; why and how consciousness arises; back to the cortex; the hard problem; and making a mind.

During the fifties and sixties, when psychoanalysis enjoyed broad popularity in the U.S., analysts were sought after as expert witnesses in criminal and child custody cases. In 1973 Joseph Goldstein, Anna Freud, and Albert Solnit published Beyond the Best Interests of the Child, which they followed with two other books on the subject. Since then, evaluations and testimony based on psychoanalytic theory have fallen out of favor in the arena of divorce litigation and are generally regarded by courts neither as scientific nor as meeting legal standards of evidence (Spinak 2007; Kapoor and Williams 2012). Despite this trend, a small number of contemporary psychoanalysts have written about high-conflict divorces, including Donner (2006), Ehrlich (2014), Galatzer-Levy, Kraus, and Galatzer-Levy (2009), Gunsberg and Hymowitz (2005), Shopper (2005), and Demby (2009, 2017). These authors have used psychoanalytic concepts and theory to deepen our understanding of the intrapsychic and 160

Post-pandemic society seems to be coming apart at the seams (Pivnick 2021; Davoine 2022). Anomie, an insurrection in the U.S., and polarized political discourse evince the group regression and search for authoritarian leadership that Freud (1921) depicted over a century ago. How can we reknit our social cohesion while preserving our sanity as differentiated 165 individuals? Can psychoanalysis help society meet this challenge? Can technology better help us change how we function? Is technology a suitable substitute for a more idealized leader?

While it is a psychoanalytic truism that the analyst's reverie helps transform preconceived sensations and expectations into thinkable thought (Bion 1962), thinking with evocatively conceived objects helps computer scientists transform the operations of their machines into manageable algorithms that change both our perceptions of the world and the actions we take to modify it (Turkle 2011). Together, analysts and computer technology modify thinking and inform important aspects of our body politic. Internalized as large “groups-in-the mind” composed of many smaller groups and individuals and their mediated relations with one another, technologically mediated groups undoubtedly contribute to our psychic functioning (Eng and Han 2000; Hassinger and Pivnick 2022; Pivnick and Hassinger 2023). But are they prosocial?

When beginning her career, the psychoanalyst and MIT professor of psychology Sherry Turkle realized that since “real life is lived with thought and feeling joined” she could shed light on both social-emotional life and scientific thinking by studying how people change through interacting with technology—and how technology in turn changes how we think about ourselves and our increasingly complex world. In her recent memoir, The Empathy Diaries, she shares her dire conclusion: our technology is leading us to severely devalue personal connection and empathy.

This cogent, lively memoir transports us through Turkle's life and loves from 1948 to 1985 to show how she used her personal human experience to derive theoretical insights about how minds and machines mold new ways of thinking and being. During her early years in Brooklyn, she was fascinated by a hidden world of family secrets revealed in her visits to objects stored in a cabinet she came to call “the memory closet.” Several of these articles were photographs of the father her mother had divorced. Rarely making an appearance in her life, he clearly prioritized saving money on child support over caring for his daughter. He had been a chemist and by her account a bit of a “mad-scientist type” who experimented on her when she was too young to protest. Her mother erased all trace of him when she divorced him and remarried. Sherry's sudden name change, which her mother enjoined Sherry to keep secret at school, set her off on the lifelong detective adventure depicted in this book. The objects hidden from her view became sequestered internally as well as externally, but her handling of the photos and other mementos helped her think about the unthinkable.

How can we build bridges between the keen insights of psychoanalysis and ordinary, familiar experiences, so that professional audiences, curious students, and an interested lay population can all be intrigued? To my 173 mind, the process begins with one foot firmly planted in psychoanalytic concepts and the other rooted in stories from clinical practice and everyday life. The benefits of this approach seem clear, yet in my opinion few achieve it successfully. The challenge is to convey the meaning of psychoanalytic concepts clearly and concisely, sacrificing none of their nuance and complexity, while at the same time vividly demonstrating their therapeutic application. I am happy to report that a new book, When the Garden Isn't Eden, by Kerry L. Malawista, Linda G. Kanefield, and Anne J. Adelman, succeeds admirably.

If we want to encourage interdisciplinary exchanges and a wider appreciation of psychoanalytic thinking, we need to engage both hearts and minds. The stories in this book illustrate and explain key analytic concepts, among them projection, projective identification, and attunement. Vivid stories are where abstract concepts and momentary experience can most readily meet.

Books that attempt this feat can fail in several ways. Either the stories are poorly chosen or only vaguely illustrative, or the analytic concepts are overloaded with references or inaccurately explained. When the Garden Isn't Eden avoids all these pitfalls. Its personal and compelling vignettes draw us into the meanings of somatic memory, the uncanny, identification, separation anxiety, mourning, and many other fundamental concepts. Each chapter features a lived experience that highlights the utility of psychoanalytic theory. The reader is drawn into appreciating how a psychoanalytic concept can inform the clinician's moment-by-moment perceptions and responses. Sometimes significant points are implied, rather than stated. For example, by toggling between foundational works (e.g., Freud 1900, quoted on pp. 84-85) or current studies (e.g., Davies 2018, quoted on p. 84) and a clinical example, the authors affirm and honor the relevance of all three as sources of applicable insight. This respectful attitude has maximal impact because it is demonstrated, rather than stated. The authors also evince an implicit but steadfast avoidance of binary thinking. Their perspective is neither intrapersonal nor interpersonal; it is both. The vignettes highlight the effectiveness of verbal interpretations and affective attunement. The clinicians are often both kind and direct. This anti-binary position is powerfully communicated in the authors’ writing style, which connects abstract concepts with experiences from their clinical practices and personal lives. On My Way

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau  

Ilove to play with words, their multiple meanings, their etymology, and their evocative power. I wonder about my sentences: Do they sound right? Is what they say true? Do they inspire the next sentence, the next paragraph, and eventually lead to the article, story, or novel I had in mind? I go over a text many times, cutting here, adding there, listening to the melody and rhythm, and changing words when they don't fit the music. In each revision I notice something to improve that previously escaped me—as if what is meant to be expressed were still halfway in my mind and would only slowly make its way out on paper—the place where the errors occur.

What is this particular pleasure in writing that I share with almost all who have written in this column? Starting a new writing project always feels challenging, but mostly I anticipate enjoying what I will be doing. It's like falling in love with the subject I'm approaching. I know, only if there is Eros and passion in what I do, only if I engage with love and care, will it be good. I usually start out with an idea that has sparked and now preoccupies my interest, at first gingerly feeling my way into it, then proceeding more vigorously and at times writing as if in a fever (only to later discard much of it), and eventually continuing more steadily, as if now moving in familiar territory. At this point I believe my psychoanalytic text will achieve what I was hoping for, and then writing is just enjoyable. By contrast, when I work in fiction such familiarity would be delusive, because if the writing proceeds too smoothly it might be flat and miss the vibrancy of an unconscious pulse that pushes imagination—mine, and the reader's as well.

To bring something new into the world is both laborious and exciting (to “give birth” to a book is a well-known trope). Whether I write a scientific paper or a short story, a theoretical book or a novel, sublimated procreation provides pleasure after all! But there is something else: When I

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Social Justice Activism as Interpretation in a Loewaldian World

Jyoti Rao

Jyoti Rao

1777 Borel Place, Suite 440
San Mateo, CA 94402
Email: jmrao@icloud.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-9143-9638

Jyoti Rao  

activism, social justice, interpretation, ethics, Loewald, creativity, social transference, transference

At a time when many questions are arising about the nexus between psychoanalysis and social justice, the writings of Hans Loewald open an avenue for broadened conceptualizations of psychoanalytic activity and the role of interpretation within it. The pursuit of social justice, it is argued, is integral to psychoanalytic ethics, and the relation between activists and society can be formulated in Loewaldian terms. Using Loewald, and considering case examples from social justice informed advocacy, direct action, and protest speech in AIDS activism, social justice activism can be understood as a spontaneously emergent psychoanalytic interpretation delivered by activists to their social surround, effectively accomplishing multiple forms of therapeutic action. The therapeutic action includes a working through in two phases of the negative social transference, a concept proposed here to elaborate a mechanism for the transformation, through the interpretive aspects of activism, of psychic material directed toward marginalized subjects and those expressing marginalized subjective positions. Resistance to social justice activism is examined using the forms of resistance identified by Freud.

[[RunningHead]]

Distance and Relation: Emerging from Embeddedness in the Other

Donnel Stern

Donnel Stern

24 East 82nd Street, Suite 1B
New York, NY 10028
Email: donnelstern@gmail.com

Donnel Stern  

separateness, embeddedness, merger, fusion, otherness, enactment, witnessing, oneness, psychic distance

Inspired by an essay by Martin Buber (1950), and then by the work of Ernest Schachtel (1959) on the idea of “embeddedness” and emergence from it, this essay is an account of the role of “distance” or “separateness” in clinical psychoanalytic work. We tend to assume that the capacity to appreciate otherness is always already present. We often lose track of the necessity to “set the other at a distance” (Buber), the prerequisite for emergence from embeddedness in the other. The entire process—i.e., setting the other at a distance and then emerging from embeddedness in the other—must take place over and over again in any treatment, and in both directions: patients must disembed from analysts, but it is just as necessary for analysts to disembed from patients. It is the emergence from embeddedness that allows the analyst's appreciation of the patient's otherness. Embeddedness in the other is discussed as mutual enactment. This use of these phenomena in treatment is articulated in the theory of witnessing presented elsewhere in recent years (Stern 2009, 2012, 2022b, in press). A detailed clinical illustration is presented.

Life in Common: On the Enigma of US

Francisco González

Francisco González

582 Market Street Suite, #305
San Francisco, CA 94104
Email: f.j.g@comcast.net

Francisco González  

I will start, right off, in good psychoanalytic manner, by looking backwards: some thirty-five years ago in this case, to the words of someone I suppose we could call an unquestioned master of psychoanalysis, André Green in 1986:

Rigidly maintaining its classical stance, psychoanalysis could on the one hand attach itself to an embalmed and stiffened corpse, failing to pursue a critical evaluation of its theories as challenged by present practice. In this case it would be pledged to the mere safeguarding of its acquisitions, without ever calling into question the theory sustaining them. The alternative is a psychoanalysis which, periodically renewing itself, strives to extend its range, to subject its concepts to radical rethinking, to commit itself to self-criticism. In which case it must run the risks entailed by such self-examination, from which the best as well as the worst may emerge [pp. 286-287].

“Present practice,” in our current moment, is confronted with a series of social ruptures that have deeply unsettled the supposed sanctity of the consulting room and challenged institutional psychoanalysis to “extend its range.” If such disturbances have the uncanny potential to make what has remained invisible finally appear, troubling what had long been taken as the unquestioned and natural order of things and revealing, through the disruption of that placid, taken-for-granted surface, the mechanisms and the history that lie behind it, then we live in a time of seemingly unlimited potential. The disruptions have come fast and furious. If the political upheaval of 2016 made painfully evident just how violently riven we are as a nation, what has become increasingly clear is that the election of

Two Cheers for Austin Ratner: The Virtues and Defects of the Psychoanalyst's Aversion to Proof

The Psychoanalyst's Aversion to Proof. By Austin Ratner. New York: IPBooks, 2019, xiv + 290 pp., $29.95 paperback.

Peter Rudnytsky

Peter Rudnytsky

P.O. Box 117130
Gainesville, FL 32611
Email: plr@ufl.edu

Peter Rudnytsky   Professor of English, University of Florida; Head of the Department of Academic and Professional Affairs and Chair, Committee on Confidentiality, American Psychoanalytic Association.

I

With its title that grabs the reader by the lapels, Austin Ratner has written a bristling porcupine of a book that addresses a host of issues important to the psychoanalytic community. A graduate of The Johns Hopkins School of Medicine and an award-winning novelist, Ratner was introduced to the world of psychoanalysis as a toddler at the Hanna Perkins Nursery School in Cleveland and has had a personal analysis, in which a central theme was the death of his father at the age of twenty-nine, eleven days before Austin's third birthday. The odyssey that began in Ratner's childhood has been crowned by his recent appointment as editor of The American Psychoanalyst, the public face of our organization. If it cannot be denied that The Psychoanalyst's Aversion to Proof has the defects of its considerable virtues, Ratner is nonetheless to be wholeheartedly commended both for the vigor of his style and for mounting an impassioned defense of psychoanalysis that is deeply rooted in the soil of his lived experience.

Rounded off by a brief introduction and conclusion, the body of Ratner's book is divided into four parts, the first three of which—“Freud's Aversion to Proof: A Case Study,” “The Legacy of Freud's Aversion to Proof,” and “The Postmodern Aversion to Proof”—set forth his diagnosis of the problem, while the fourth, “Solutions to the Problem of Proof Aversion,” outlines his proposed remedy. Lynne Zeavin

Lynne Zeavin

80 University Place, Fifth Floor

https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3427-6710

Lynne Zeavin  

anxiety, countertransference, phantasy, mourning, attention

Interpretation remains relevant in contemporary psychoanalysis and serves a crucial linking function between patient and analyst. Interpretation provides an important link with temporalities: the time of the analytic hour and the time of the patient's history as it unfolds in the present. Analysis, it is argued, is bounded by time and loss. Two case vignettes, presented from a Kleinian perspective, exemplify these propositions.

The Play of Mourning

Steven Cooper

Steven Cooper

5 West 86th Street, 11E
New York, NY 10023
Email: stevencooper03@gmail.com

Steven Cooper  

play, mourning, countertransference, transference, unconscious, separation, internalization, interpretation, intersubjectivity, enactment

Playing is a form of responsiveness that involves a shift from more formal interpretation about defense, unconscious fantasy, or transference to one that employs humor or irony regarding the content of fantasy or poses a more direct confrontation between internal fantasy and external reality. Playing is differentiated from more formal interpretation by the analytic couple's intensity of affective expression, the idiomatic language used to express affect or ideas, or the analyst's more personally revealing reaction to the patient's recruitment of him as an internal object. Two clinical vignettes show how play emphasizes experiences of loss and waste that have been enacted in the patient's life and often in transference-countertransference engagement. Through newly discovered forms of play, these processes are occurring now in real time between patient and analyst and less through frozen memorialization of what never was.

[[RunningHead]]

Interpretation: Voice of the Field

Donnel Stern

Donnel Stern

20 West 74th Street
New York, NY 10023
Email: donnelstern@gmail.com

Donnel Stern  

interpretation, relational theory, field, theory, interpersonal theory, therapeutic action, witnessing

To patients, the most memorable moments in psychoanalytic treatment are seldom the contents of the analyst's interpretations, but the feeling of being understood. Interpretations are most meaningful not because of what they say but because each one is evidence that the analyst, who generally becomes someone of great significance to the patient, knows the patient more than before the interpretation was made. As a result of this process of “witnessing” patients not only know and feel—they also “know and feel that they know and feel.” They can feel their roles in authoring their own experience. Therapeutic action results: patients “come into possession of themselves.” Interpretations are the outcome of shifts in the interpersonal field, which reveal this new freedom to think and feel. This new freedom allows the creation of the analyst's interpretations, which therefore serve as a sign of a new way of being in one another's presence that has now become possible between analyst and patient. Field shifts are jointly created, without conscious intention, and interpretations arise from these shifts. Thus, interpretations are not really created independently by the mind of the analyst, but are instead the voice of the field. A clinical vignette illustrates these ideas.

Treating Psychosis Today: A Lacanian Take

Stijn Vanheule

Stijn Vanheule

H. Dunantlaan 2
Ghent 9000
Belgium
Email: stijn.vanheule@ugent.be
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-8580-5809

Stijn Vanheule  

psychosis, father function, Lacan, recovery, hallucination

This paper examines the principal ideas from Jacques Lacan's psychoanalytic theory of psychosis. According to Lacan's theory in the 1950s, the central organizing element of symbolically organized mental life, the Name-of-the-Father, is missing in psychosis. That theory changes with later conceptual developments in Lacan's work that focus on the incompleteness of symbolic functioning. This connects with how, in his works from the late 1960s and the 1970s, Lacan embraces the idea of a fundamental non-rapport and symbolic non-existence at the basis of mental life. In a second step, the paper explores what the Lacanian model of psychosis implies with regard to ethical positioning, addressing the unconscious, handling transference, and crisis and stability in psychosis. A clinical case discussion focuses on a yearlong therapeutic trajectory with a young man with Down's syndrome who suffered from psychotic experiences.

[[RunningHead]]

It's About Time: Loewald, Lacan, and Après-Coup in America

Alfred Margulies

Alfred Margulies

1493 Cambridge Street
Cambridge MA 02139-1099
Email: alfred_margulies@hms.harvard.edu

Alfred Margulies  

Lacan, Loewald, Heidegger, après-coup, Time, Being, existence, future

Martin Heidegger's thought deeply influenced both Hans Loewald and Jacques Lacan, the catalyst they seemed to have been waiting for. For Loewald, Heidegger's ontological centrality of time to Being-in-the-World would bridge to Freud's centrality of transference to the analytic process, thereby operationalizing transference as a prism of time. In revealing the interwoven correlatives of present-past-future, how they bootstrap one another phenomenologically, Loewald also revealed a spiral of recursive meaning (in essence, après-coup) that draws us into the future, “the something more” of existence. In parallel, through his recognition of the power of après-coup, Lacan rescued from obscurity Freud's profound conception of Nachträglichkeit, or the spiral and causal force of unfolding meaning. Lacan was now situated to bring après-coup in conjunction with Heidegger's Being-in-the-World, with time interwoven into all aspects of existence, thereby underpinning, too, language and the Symbolic Order. By reading Freud through Heidegger and then creating their brilliant syntheses, Loewald and Lacan, through their striking sameness and differences, illuminate the nature of the unconscious, of memory and meaning, of the spiral of time, and of existence itself.

[[RunningHead]]

JAPA Prizes for 2022

Avgi Saketopoulou has been awarded the 2022 JAPA Prize for her paper “Anti-Racist Racism: Trauma, Traumatism, Traumatophilia” (70/6, pp. 1071-1109).

The 2022 JAPA New Author Prize has been jointly awarded to Jennifer Guittard for her paper “When the Good Object Is Also a Thief: A Memoir of Adoption” (70/1, pp. 39-76) and Jamey Hecht for his paper “Bion at the Crossroads: A Contrarian Reading of ‘On Arrogance’” (70/4, pp. 613-636).

Special Mention

Beginning in 2018 Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris began a series of conversations with an international group of psychoanalysts concerning the recently published twelve-volume Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott. Ultimately, they invited these analysts to each submit an essay focusing on a single volume of the collected works. These essays were published in JAPA in sequence over a three-year period during which we were able to witness Winnicott slowly coming to preserve his profound love of Freud while simultaneously establishing his own radical voice and innovations. This award is to be shared with the authors of these essays, all of whom acknowledge with Winnicott that play is an engine of therapeutic action and serves as the generator of an experience that enables both patient and analyst to feel alive, present, and known.

[[RunningHead]]

Lacan and the Americans: Part 2

Darian Leader

Darian Leader

Hay House
London W1J8NR
UNITED KINGDOM
Email: darian@cfar.uk

Darian Leader  

In an earlier article (Leader 2021a) I sketched out some of the prejudices and mistaken beliefs about psychoanalysis in the States that are ubiquitous in Lacanian circles. Dialogue between Lacanians and the practitioners and theorists of what Sergio Benvenuto (2021) has called “Austro-German psychoanalysis transplanted to the USA”—to acknowledge the contribution of emigré analysts—had been historically stalled by competing interests and a rather staggering amount of misinformation circulating on both sides (though mostly the Lacanian one).

Americans tended to be portrayed as stupid, greedy proponents of extreme forms of ego psychology, which was itself seen as an avatar of market capitalism and conformist politics. The vital, rich traditions of American psychoanalytic thinking that offered critiques of ego psychology, explored the effect of capitalism on human subjectivity, and developed alternative research programs were just completely ignored, and authors cited only if an idea could be seen as a weak approximation of something Lacan had himself said.

These prejudices were an effect not only of the antagonism between Lacan and his professional adversaries but also of the contemporary culture wars of the 1950s and the anti-U.S. propaganda of Gaullism in France (Simpson-Berryman 1967b; Guilbaud 1983; Lebovics 1999). Lacan's later lecturing visits to the States were hardly helpful here, and the contrasting reactions to his interventions bring out nicely the polarities at play: for some, divine encrypted wisdom requiring reading groups and endless years of study to decipher; for others, a muddled mixture of dogma and aphorism that was more about theater and spectacle than psychoanalytic research. Both of these perspectives would presumably be nuanced today when we factor in the question of power relations both within and between psychoanalytic groups.

Founder and member, Center for Freudian Analysis and Research.

Invited paper, received September 1, 2023.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Becoming and Being a Subject of Sexual Desire

Lynne Harkless

Lynne Harkless

4601 Ponce de Leon Boulevard, Suite 310
Coral Gables, FL 33146
Email: lharkless1@aol.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-1937-5772

Lynne Harkless  

desire, gender, sexuality

Emergent erotic desire, it is proposed, becomes represented in the mind and body through identification with caregivers as subjects of desire. Here the focus within desire is on erotic desire for another person, both desire for and the wish to be desirable to particular others. Children are seen to identify with caregivers' modes of embodying erotic desire for others (including ways of moving, dressing, relating, and so on that they fantasize as expressing erotic desire for others) in order to represent, psychically and bodily, their emerging erotic desire. These identifications—desire identifications—have a role in representing desire for others that is comparable to the role played by gender identifications in the representation of gender. Embodiments of desire for others, it is argued, are distinguishable (momentarily) from embodiments of masculinity and femininity. These embodiments of desire are routinely characterized, erroneously, as masculinity or femininity by caregivers and culture, and this misrecognition of desire for others as gender is traumatic to the self in its formation as a subject of desire. An extended clinical case is presented to illustrate how desire identifications might arise in the analytic dyad, relationally, bodily, and erotically in the transference-countertransference.

[[RunningHead]]

Limits on Love: A Critical Discourse Analysis of Psychoanalytic Texts

Faithlynn Morris

Faithlynn Morris

635 West 165th Street, 4th & 6th floor
New York, NY 10032
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4902-5845
Email: faithlynn.morris@gmail.com

Faithlynn Morris  

love, language, power, Black feminism, critical, psychoanalysis

Critical discourse methodology and a Black feminist lens were used to examine the discourse of contemporary psychoanalysis, specifically to investigate the relationship between language, love, and power. Findings of the analysis include the following. The discourse encourages engagement with linguistic shortcuts, wherein concepts such as oppression and bigotry are used as conduits to discuss intrapsychic experiences. The discourse frames whiteness as the center of experience and marginalizes Blackness, Indigenousness, and other nonwhite perspectives. The discourse is vague about what constitutes love and what place love should have in clinical work. Ultimately, the discourse of the texts in this analysis suggests there is more work to do, more areas in the discipline to disrupt, and more love to give.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Red Thread: Explorations of the Unconscious Sense of Guilt

Sarah Ackerman

Sarah Ackerman

5 Hunter Lane
Hanover, NH 03755
Email: Ackerman@mmyahoo.com

Sarah Ackerman  

unconscious sense of guilt, Sigmund Freud, oedipus complex, compulsion to repeat, après-coup

The concept of an “unconscious sense of guilt” bedevils Freud throughout his life, rearing its head in at least twenty-four of his major works and working behind the scenes in many others. In a sense, we can see Freud's oeuvre, and psychoanalysis more generally, as a discourse of unconscious guilt. While Freud frames the oedipus complex as the central defining dynamic of human experience, the unconscious sense of guilt is arguably the underbelly that both precedes and exceeds that complex. By unraveling a range of complexities within Freud's conceptualization of unconscious guilt, we will come to see that guilt is an unavoidable by-product of the human condition, intrinsically interwoven with libidinal desire.

[[RunningHead]]

“How His Hair is Growing Thin!”: On the Emotional Significance of Male Pattern Hair Loss

Tom Wooldridge

Tom Wooldridge

536 Mission Street
San Francisco, CA 94105
Email: twooldridge@ggu.edu

Tom Wooldridge  

hair loss, male psychology, masculinity, castration anxiety

Hair is a powerful symbol of individual and group identity: physical and therefore personal yet public rather than private. Despite the fact that male pattern hair loss causes men considerable emotional distress, its unconscious emotional significance is almost entirely unrepresented in the psychoanalytic literature, with a few notable exceptions. After a review of the sociological literature on male pattern hair loss and the relatively sparse psychoanalytic literature on hair and hair loss, four interlocking perspectives on hair loss in men are elaborated: (1) hair as the locus of maternal desire, (2) hair as evoking the helplessness of early infancy, (3) hair loss as a symbol of aging and death, and (4) hair loss as evoking paternal identifications and experiences with the paternal function. These ideas are illustrated with reference to T. S. Eliot's “The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock” and with a clinical case.

[[RunningHead]]

Thinking in a Marrow Bone: Embodiment in Vajrayana Buddhism and Psychoanalysis

Kristin Fiorella

Kristin Fiorella

1700 Pierce Street, Suite 306
San Francisco, CA 94115
Email: kristin.fiorella@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0001-9976-733X

Kristin Fiorella  

embodiment, Vajrayana Buddhism, nondualistic, Vajra body, intercorporeal communication, mind/body dissociation

Nondualistic conceptions of the body in Vajrayana Buddhism and some schools of Zen potentially extend the range for imagining and conceptualizing the analyst's body. They add dimension to psychoanalytic explorations of nonverbal, body-to-body communication in the analytic dyad. Vajrayana Buddhism posits that the body that we have from the point of view of the conceptual mind is not our only body. The body that we are, known as the Vajra body, is experientially available only when the conceptual mind is relaxed. The Vajra body suggests an aspect of embodiment that upsets distinctions of subject/object, mind/body, and internal/external. From a Vajrayana perspective, some psychoanalytic views of the body blunt our bodies' potential sensitivity and then theorize in response to this objectified body. Thinking that the body is primitive and requires the transformative capacities of the mind distorts modes of sentience that are bodily. From a Vajrayana perspective, this assumption locks in an overreliance on the conceptual mind that is self-perpetuating. It also inhibits a useful, though at times disturbing potential for intercorporeal communication. A detailed clinical example illustrates how these nondualistic ideas about embodiment might influence analytic work.

responseSet.authorMast: Lauren Smith,  Katie Aafjes-van Doorn and  Tracy A. Prout

responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Changing Attitudes Toward Evidence-Based Psychodynamic Psychotherapy

Lauren Smith , Katie Aafjes-van Doorn and Tracy Prout

Tracy Prout

225 East 82th Street, Apt. 7D
New York, NY 10028
Email: lsmith5@mail.yu.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0022-7411

https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2584-5897

https://orcid.org/0000-0002-3650-5890

Tracy Prout  

evidence-based, psychodynamic, graduate training, attitude change

The past four decades have seen a consistent move toward evidence-based clinical practices (EBPs). EBPs include empirical research on specific treatments, clinical expertise, and client values and preferences (American Psychological Association Presidential Task Force on Evidence-Based Practice, 2006). EBPs are often thought to be synonymous with cognitive-behavioral therapy interventions, and a widespread misconception persists that psychodynamic therapies (PDTs) are not evidence-based. It is important, then, that beliefs and attitudes toward EBPs and evidenced-based PDTs be addressed in postgraduate training so that students can consider evidence-based interventions from a range of theoretical orientations. Training graduate students in psychodynamic interventions is associated with positive treatment effects and a strong therapeutic alliance (Hilsenroth et al. 2006). Thus, it may be expected that graduate training courses that center around evidence-based PDTs may help the next generation of therapists become well-rounded practitioners.

Our study explores the impact of graduate courses in evidence-based PDTs on students' attitudes toward EBP and evidence-based PDT.

Lauren Smith, Intake Coordinator, IMPACT Psychological Services, Ferkauf Graduate School of Psychology, Yeshiva University. Katie Aafjes-van Doorn, Ferkauf Graduate School of Psychology, Yeshiva University. Tracy A. Prout, Ferkauf Graduate School of Psychology, Yeshiva University.

Submitted for publication March 28, 2021; revised May 17, 2021; accepted May 23, 2021.

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky  

Reconsiderations

Within contemporary psychoanalysis, analytic identities, core conceptual and developmental concepts, and cross-cultural issues are being radically re-envisioned. In this section, we are pleased to present five diverse reviews offering important reflections on a range of theoretical topics that explore these themes and invite reconsideration, exploration, and interrogation of prior views.

In The Unconscious: Theory, Research, and Clinical Implications, Julia-Matthews Bellinger writes in her review, authors Joel Weinberger and Valentina Stoycheva “aim to summarize and synthesize a now-extensive literature from cognitive neuroscience, experimental psychology, and social psychology that demonstrates the importance of unconscious processes in human mental functioning, and then to suggest its implications for psychotherapy practice.” Weinberger and Stoycheva are both researchers and clinicians, and before psychoanalytic training Matthews-Bellinger earned a Ph.D. in Neurobiology and Behavior (along with an M.D.); so authors and reviewer, together, are well-equipped to guide clinicians through the growing body of research on unconscious processes. Matthews-Bellinger's review follows Weinberger and Stoycheva as they trace shifts in scientific focus on unconscious processes, from a period of relative neglect; through an era of increased attention to “‘the normative unconscious‘—largely automatic, nonconflictual cognitive processes—to the past forty years, when developments in artificial intelligence and computational neuroscience have yielded ”several attempts to model how the brain functions.“ For psychoanalysts, the value of Weinberger and Stoycheva's book lies mainly in its treatment of research from fields adjacent to psychoanalysis; Matthews-Bellinger notes the absence, from this volume, of research on the dynamic unconscious conducted by psychoanalysts (e.g., Howard Shevrin, Linda Brakel, 319

Julia Matthews-Bellinger

Julia Matthews-Bellinger

19 Fair Oaks Park
Needham, MA 02492

Julia Matthews-Bellinger   Training and Supervising Analyst and faculty, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute and Society; Associate Professor of Clinical Psychiatry, UMass Chan Medical School.

This volume1 is the third in the Psychoanalysis and Psychological Science Series edited by Elliot Jurist. The authors, Joel Weinberger and Valentina Stoycheva, aim to summarize and synthesize a now extensive literature from cognitive neuroscience, experimental psychology, and social psychology that demonstrates the importance of unconscious processes in human mental functioning, and then to suggest its implications for psychotherapy practice. As the authors acknowledge, this is an ambitious undertaking with the goal of reaching “an understanding of unconscious processes” (p. ix). As the psychoanalyst and researcher Howard Shevrin (2000) has observed, the domains of cognitive science and psychotherapy remain difficult to bridge:

The experimental psychologist and the psychoanalyst live and breathe in totally different ambiences. … the clinician is primarily concerned with singularity … with the uniqueness of the person on the couch, … the experimental psychologist is concerned with generality, the functions and processes shared across people [p. 256].

Shevrin went on to hope that these disparate disciplines can consider the gaps between worlds “as a challenge and opportunity and not simply as a pitfall to be avoided” (p. 258). This volume is an effort of the kind Shevrin had in mind, and aims toward a worthy but difficult goal.

The authors are both practicing therapists, and Weinberger has founded a consulting firm that works with a variety of groups to understand how implicit cognition shapes our perception of information. Based on the text, I am compelled to assume that their therapy practices are built on cognitive-behavioral therapy and its derivatives. References to psychodynamic thought are few and lacking in depth.

1Winner of the American Board and Academy of Psychoanalysis Book Prize (Theory), American Psychological Association, 2021.325

What are the beliefs and aims of contemporary psychoanalysts? How do they see themselves and their profession? How have older analysts changed their theoretical thinking and psychoanalytic practice? How do younger analysts view their life's work? Who are the most influential psychoanalysts past and present? Where is the profession headed? In short, how has psychoanalysis, the “impossible profession,” changed over the last century? Responses to these crucial questions appear in the aptly titled Psychoanalytic Credos: Personal and Professional Journeys of Psychoanalysts, edited by Jill Salberg, a clinical associate professor, consultant, and supervisor at the NYU Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

Years ago, Salberg began assigning her students articles focusing on psychoanalysts' personal credos. She credits the idea to Emmanuel Ghent's “Credo: The Dialectics of One-Person and Two-Person Psychologies” (1989). The exercise worked so well that Salberg conceived of the present volume, comprising reprints of noteworthy articles and newly commisioned essays. Salberg wanted to include a broad theoretical spectrum of views that may be called the American counterpart to the British “middle school” or “independent tradition,” with most analysts identifying themselves as relational, object relational, contemporary Freudian, or Kleinian/Bionian—and a few who defy any affiliation. The contributors include both psychoanalytic superstars and rising stars. Twenty-seven essays appear, spanning five decades: 1960s (Sheldon Bach and Haydée Faimberg); 1970s (Ricardo Bernardi, Christopher Bollas, Stephen Mitchell, Jay Greenberg, Nancy McWilliams, and Michael Parsons); 1980s (Lewis Aron, Jessica Benjamin, Steven Cooper, Adrienne Harris, Thomas Ogden, Donna Orange, Jill Salberg, and Joyce Slochower); 1990s (Mary Brady, Ken Corbett, Elizabeth Corpt, Dianne Elise, Bruce Reis, Stephen Seligman, and Melanie Suchet); and 2000s (Stephen Hartman, Ilana Laor, Avgi Saketopoulou, and Chana Ullman). I vividly remember the first time I read about the Paris Psychosomatic School's understanding of psychosomatic patients via their characteristic way of thinking. I encountered it in a short introduction by Marilia Aisenstein and Claude Smadja (2010) to a paper by Pierre Marty (1952), one of the founders of the Psychosomatic School. In describing the cure of a patient with back problems, Marty found

a painful inhibition of thought that, as for back-pain syndromes, he thought of as related to a failure of the dreamwork and therefore of the process of representation itself. Thus it was not a question of looking for the content to give sense to the somatic symptoms but rather of observing the inhibitions or failures of psychic elaboration that precede or accompany them [p. 343].1

This description not only broadened my understanding of a psychic phenomenon I'd been trying to comprehend (i.e., language action), but also 1This profound insight, clearly stated, is similar to what post-Bionions have described, but with a more well-defined analytic purpose than the use of intuitive countertransference among some post-Bionions (see Busch 2019).340

Nathan Szajnberg   Former Freud Professor, The Hebrew University; Training Analyst, Israel and International Psychoanalytical Societies.

Psychoanalysts are interested in China. Or at least in Chinese therapists. Chinese therapists are interested in something they call “psychoanalysis,” which may be similar to what Western psychoanalysts call psychoanalysis, to the degree that there is any consensus about what that is. Western analysts should therefore be interested in learning about Chinese psychotherapy, or psychiatry, or mental institutions (or what Chinese have called “mental health”). Emily Baum's book is a contribution to the history of psychiatry and mental ailments in China. Her premise is this: until the early twentieth century, Chinese kept their insane at home, where they were “treated by healers who claimed no specialized knowledge …”; per Baum, this changed in the period between the early 1900s and 1937, as “psychiatric ideas and institutions began to influence longstanding beliefs about the proper treatment for the mentally ill”; madness, she argues, “was transformed in the Chinese imagination into ‘mental illness‘” (back cover blurb).

This book comes with impressive credentials, including the author's Fulbright, Rockefeller, and National Endowment for the Humanities awards, as well as publication by the University of Chicago Press. She is knowledgeable about Chinese medicine. We, a native Chinese psychologist trained in England (YW) and a psychoanalyst/psychiatrist (NS), however, are writing for psychoanalytic/psychiatric audiences and will speak to those readers. Baum purports to demonstrate that “madness” is a Western construct that was used by the Chinese government out of a “political desire for sovereignty, strength and social control” (p. 45). But we will use her data and data from other China historians to show that these do not support her hypotheses. Further, we will argue that to understand the sociopolitical milieu of her starting point, 1907, one must be 345

The field of psychoanalysis is at a crossroads with regard to our theoretical understanding of gender and sexuality. The notion that “anatomy is destiny” (Freud 1912, p. 189) has largely been debunked—or at least recontextualized—giving way to more nuanced theories of masculinity and femininity. For the past three decades, Michael Diamond has been at the forefront of this dialectic, writing prolifically about men, masculinity, and fatherhood. His new book, Masculinity and Its Discontents, offers an integrative developmental perspective on male gender identity through a critical examination of three historical waves of psychoanalytic gender theorizing. It is an ambitious project, one that partly eludes Diamond given the dynamic complexities of gender development and its various biological, familial, and cultural influences.

Diamond's command of psychoanalytic theory is truly impressive, allowing him to provide a concise but highly comprehensive overview of how psychoanalytic thought has evolved to account for male psychic development. The first wave of gender theory is organized around Freud's oedipal complex and the idea that a boy forms his masculine identity by defensively identifying with his father in an attempt to ward off castration anxiety associated with incestuous wishes and related patricidal impulses. Diamond is rightfully critical of the second wave of gender theory, commonly associated with the work of Ralph Greenson (1968) and Robert Stoller (1968), who, in studying “transsexuals,” argued that for a boy to establish a “normal,” healthy sense of masculinity he must first disidentify from his mother in order to counteridentify with his father. Diamond explores the conceptual and empirical limitations of the “disidentification hypothesis” and persuasively refutes much of the evidence supporting Greenson's and Stoller's argument that boys are inherently “proto-feminine” and must renounce their feminine identifications with their mothers if they are to achieve a healthy sense of masculinity.

When a Child Grieves. By Corinne Masur. Oxford: Phoenix, 2021, vi + 239 pp., $39.95 paperback.

Mali Mann

Mali Mann

1202 Woodview Terrace
Los Altos, CA 94024
Email: mannm@stanford.edu

Mali Mann   Training and Supervising Analyst and Geographic Child Analyst Supervisor, San Francisco Center for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Professor, Stanford University School of Medicine, Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Science.

The loss of a parent permanently changes the life of a child. It is considered one of the most adverse childhood challenges a child might experience. Indeed, in Western societies the effects of death on a child have not received adequate attention. However, in a contemporary nuclear family, the loss of a parent is more traumatic than in the traditional extended families of past generations. In the former, most parents try to protect their children from any comprehension of death and dying. As Erna Furman (1974) has noted, only in childhood does death deprive a person of so much opportunity to love and be loved, and pose such a difficult task of adaptation.

In When a Child Grieves: Psychoanalytic Understanding and Technique, Corinne Masur takes on on topics that remain difficult to broach. The book is both a personal account and a comprehensively researched study of childhood bereavement after losing a parent. Masur shares her own experience of parental loss and examines her inner life to offer an authentic perspective on childhood bereavement. The book, unique in the child analytic literature, focuses on understanding children who, suddenly facing the death of a parent or the loss of loved ones, are tossed into an unknown universe of grief. The loss of a parent is devastating, and each child has a unique way of reacting to the loss.

The book's Part I, “Theory,” addresses the history and theoretical underpinnings of the study of bereavement, explores the meanings and developmental themes of childhood bereavement and mourning, and examines children's capacity to understand death. Part II addresses the clinical challenges of treating childhood bereavement. There Masur presents case examples and elaborates on important clinical moments of contact with the therapist.

Masur offes a comprehensive review of psychoanalytic and developmental perspectives, historical and current, on children of different ages 361
