Kerry Malawista   Training Analyst, Contemporary Freudian Society; Co-Chair, New Directions in Writing.

In the prologue to Giving Hope, Elena Lister shares the loss of her child from leukemia and the value she found in “having conversations in order to address fears and process emotions to get through the pain to the other side” (p. 5). She deeply understands grief and loss from the inside. The entire book offers clear, wise advice to parents on initiating and navigating difficult conversations with children about illness and death. Lister, an adult, adolescent, and child psychiatrist, and Michael Schwartzman, a psychologist and psychoanalyst, stress that none of these are single conversations, but conversations that will take place, again and again, over days, weeks, and years to come. With compassion they share the common pitfalls all parents experience when broaching difficult subjects with children. They point out that while they use the word “parent” throughout, this book is for any adult helping a child through loss.

Though much of what Lister, Schwartzman, and Tate offer in this book will be intuitively understood and appreciated by clinicians, it is useful to have problem-solving approaches, accessible real-life stories, and role-playing dialogue collected in one volume. Each chapter offers summative side boxes, or “takeaways,” emphasizing important themes 368

responseSet.title: Wording the Strange
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Wording the Strange

Eugene Mahon

Eugene Mahon

8 East 96th Street
New York, NY10128
Email: ejmahon8@gmail.com

Eugene Mahon  

I think “why I write” is a delightfully impossible question: it's like asking a child why do you speak or why do you play. In the first year of life the infant is awash in a presymbolic world in which the senses are being bombarded by the strange phenomenology of experience day in, day out. Being born and being alive in that first year of life must be an “unforgettable and unrememberable” experience. I would suggest that the writer is also forever trying to bear witness to that unforgettable strangeness of birth and life and give it a “local habitation and a name,” thereby making it, if not familiar, at least a little less strange.

George Seferis famously suggested that “the poem is everywhere!” I think he meant that that cluster of words we call a poem existed, non-verbally, in the phenomenology of the world and that it is the poet's astonishment and subsequent curiosity that comes up with the mots justes to represent it with a word-flourish that seems to fit judiciously. This preamble, in other words, suggests that the writer, like the infant, is always struck by the strangeness of the human condition, a strangeness that inspires a curiosity that seeks words to make sense of that strangeness.

I want to describe writings that all sprang from my curiosity about this gestational strangeness. I remember seeing Kenneth Branagh's film, Hamlet, in 1996. In act 1, scene ii, there is a parapraxis in Hamlet's first soliloquy. What was strange about this was that in all the other viewings of Hamlet, on stage or film, that I had seen over many decades, I never noticed this striking slip of the tongue. Hamlet, referring to his father's death, says, “But two months dead—” and then immediately corrects himself, saying “nay, not so much, not two.” Corrected, or not, the parapraxis stands as does its unconscious oedipal meaning! The parapraxis suggests that Hamlet must have had the death of his father on his mind before his Uncle Claudius killed him! In case Shakespeare's use of the

responseSet.title: Integrative Individuation: An Alternative to the Separation-Individuation Model
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Integrative Individuation: An Alternative to the Separation-Individuation Model

Gurmeet Kanwal

Gurmeet Kanwal

275 Central Park West, #1W
New York, NY 10024
Email: gkanwalmd@gmail.com

Gurmeet Kanwal  

separation-individuation, integrative individuation, culture, development, self

An alternative to Mahler's separation-individuation model of child development is presented to explain differences in the development and experience of a sense of self in Indian culture and other cultures where the Western sense of individual selfhood is not seen as the goal of maturity and adulthood. In the absence of such a formulation, called here integrative individuation, the familial and relational experience of people from non-Western cultures is often misunderstood and pathologized by clinicians. Features of this non-Western sense of self include looser boundaries, different relational priorities, and greater tolerance regarding personal space. Though these differences have been commented on by scholars, a detailed developmental model has not previously been formulated.

responseSet.title: Solms's Revision of Drive Theory
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Solms's Revision of Drive Theory

Lois Oppenheim

Lois Oppenheim

181 East 73 Street, #10F
New York, NY 10021
Email: oppenheiml@montclair.edu

Lois Oppenheim  

March 1, 2023. In the commentaries on Mark Solms's “Revision of Drive Theory” (JAPA 69/6) a number of inaccuracies are to be found. It is possible in a letter such as this to reference but a few. I will therefore limit myself to some points made in the first of the two commentaries, that by Alan Sugarman.

Sugarman claims that the Solms model is “reductionistic” for focusing on brain as opposed to mental functioning. In complete agreement with Freud's definition of drive (incorrectly translated by Strachey from trieb as instinct) as the connection between mind and body, Solms adopts a dual-aspect monist perspective and revises (much as Freud himself wished to be able to do at the time) some key features of his metapsychology in function of what is known about the brain today but was not known a century ago. Solms mentions dual-aspect monism in his response to the commentaries, but it should be noted that Freud's own perspective was that of a dual-aspect monist and that Solms is in complete agreement with Freud's view of the mind as “driven by an energy of some kind” (Solms, p. 1036), which Solms defines as the very reason for being of drive—homeostasis and the energic objective of feeling: survival. In so doing, he resituates drives within the frame of necessity, satiation as opposed to pleasure, which leads from the notion of voluntary behavior to that of learning by experience and defines a “subjective aspect of ‘drive‘”: affect (Solms, p. 1052), which could hardly be more related to psychological meaning.

Next Sugarman states that “neuroscience provides only a parallelism that demonstrates the correlation between brain functioning and subjective mental experience” and claims that “no superordinate model of human functioning has yet been discovered that can encompass and integrate the neurological spheres of human experience” (p. 1142). Moreover, he states, citing George S. Klein, that “even if one agrees to define drive as arising from neurobiological sources, it ‘becomes motivational only when it is cognitively represented.’” I believe Solms would be the first to agree, and he has in fact demonstrated exactly that in explaining the value of feelings: in eliminating the erroneous dichotomy between a “‘biologized’ metapsychology and a supposedly pure psychology of ‘meaning 509

It is with a mix of delight and sadness that we present the final essay in our series on The Collected Works of D. W. Winnicott. We greatly appreciate the depth and range of reviews that our guest editors, Phillip Blumberg and Adrienne Harris, have brought to this project. Taken together, our reviewers' reactions show how Winnicott's ideas continue to guide and inform contemporary psychoanalytic thinking. Though we are sorry to see the project conclude, we are pleased to close it with a thoughtful essay by Lesley Caldwell, co-editor of the Collected Works. We are indeed grateful to Blumberg and Harris for the care with which they have overseen this exceptional project over several years.

In a book essay on virtuality, Aden Evens brings into conversation two distinctly different volumes—Hannah Zeavin's The Distance Cure: A History of Teletherapy and Richard Frankel and Victor Krebs's Human Virtuality and Digital Life: Philosophical and Psychoanalytic Investigations. Evens, an associate professor of English and creative writing at Dartmouth, brings to his work on media, the digital, and philosophy a long-standing interest in psychoanalysis that equips him to view the digital world through an analytic lens. The two books reviewed in this essay each grapple—as impartially as possible, and from varied angles—with the impact on self and other of an increasingly digital world. Fortuitously (given our recent focus on Winnicott)—as Evens notes—each work draws on Winnicott's thinking: Zeavin invokes the Winnicott whose radio addresses “appeal[ed] to the masses of forlorn mothers whose male partners [were] off fighting in World War II,” thereby beginning to “pave the way for call-in shows that dispense advice, entertainment, and wisdom for life”; and for Frankel and Krebs, Evens observes, the Winnicott whose “image of the infant as omnipotent, experiencing no difference between desire and fulfillment, provides an opportunity … to stake out a place for the virtual in the infant psyche and so to establish a human virtual.” Zeavin's approach is pragmatic, opening the notion of 513

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reflections on the JAPA Reviews of Winnicott's Collected Works

Lesley Caldwell

Lesley Caldwell

Email: l.caldwell@ucl.ac.uk

Lesley Caldwell   Co-editor, The Collected Works of D.W. Winnicott; Psychoanalysis Unit, University College London.

Thanks to Adrienne Harris and Phillip Blumberg for their inspired idea of having Winnicott's Collected Works reviewed volume by volume in JAPA. The project has resulted in a distinctive contribution to Winnicott scholarship on the history and evolution of psychoanalysis and its contemporary parameters. These review essays testify to the international interest in Winnicott's theoretical and clinical sophistication, his central place in postwar English psychoanalysis, and his later influence worldwide, but they also draw attention to his popularity now in the United States and a certain interest in previous decades that deserves systematic exploration and research.

Apart from two long-established Israeli Winnicott scholars, Ofra Eshel and Emanuel Berman, and Giuseppe Civitarese, known for his work on Bion and the Italian development of field theory, the reviewers are American. As a direct result of the Collected Works' publication in 2016, a volume of papers edited by Joseph Aguayo discussing Winnicott's visits to the U.S. in the 1960s will be published next year. It includes two earlier papers by American analysts, Francis Baudry and Nellie Thompson, which come out of a very different moment but are equally relevant today.

I hope there will be continuing detailed investigation of the American Winnicott (or Winnicotts), especially with regard to the general shifts that have occurred in psychoanalytic practice and scholarship in what may be regarded as distinctly American schools of thought.

The Project in Formation

Aden Evens   Associate Professor of English and Creative Writing, Dartmouth College; founding Co-Director, Psychoanalysis Study Group, sponsored by the Leslie Center for the Humanities, Dartmouth College.

One might imagine, embracing a Cartesian rationalism, that once one has set aside biases, rejected dogmatism, and exhausted polemics, only truth will remain. Uninfluenced by any prejudgment and dedicated to a studied neutrality, one thereby comes to see most clearly the territory under observation. This is the epistemological gambit, embraced by both Hannah Zeavin and by Richard Frankel and Victor Krebs, who hope that their books, refusing to take sides, will generate the most perspicacious account of their subject matters. Notwithstanding some curious points of contact, those subject matters are quite distinct, and even the technique of impartiality is differently deployed from one book to the other. Zeavin's The Distance Cure suspends most judgment about technologically mediated forms of therapy in order to trace the post-Freudian lineage of such therapies and ponder their potential efficacies. Frankel and Krebs's Human Virtuality and Digital Life engages, by contrast, almost wholly in evaluative analysis, examining the radical transformations of the self under conditions of digital ubiquity, but that book institutes a neutrality by insisting that every story has at least two sides, and then making sure to narrate them all.

The consequences of avoiding polemics also play out differently in the tones and theses of the two books. Zeavin's mission is anti-elitist and even anti-hierarchical, quietly celebrating therapy by mail, radio therapy, telephone hotlines, email, web-based and chat-based therapy, and even bot-based therapy because these therapies bring mental health to the 533

Louisa Hong   Adult psychoanalysis candidate, PANY; Clinical Instructor, NYU Langone Department of Psychiatry.

I was asked to review this book in 2019, shortly after its publication. One might think that the gap in time between then and now is attributable to the Covid-19 pandemic and the unprecedented seismic events, local and global, that ensued. That's partly true. The rest of the story is more flesh and bone. I had my children in these few years, and spent most of the intervening time contending with similarly cataclysmic shifts within me, amid the mind- and life-altering transformations of the pandemic.

I held off on opening this book. Though it is a mere 204 pages, its bold title and cadre of psychoanalytic luminaries on the topic of women and womanhood daunted me, particularly in the hazy, uncertain days of new motherhood. The last thing I wanted was to open up my conflicts regarding desire, ambition, and leadership, when what I needed was to feel sure about something in the new identity I'd begun to form. I'd look guiltily at the book sitting on my desk before I hurried off to chase that certainty.

When eventually I approached the book, in the “new normal,” I devoured it. Again and again. Though an academic text in some ways, it spoke to me plainly, and for once I didn't need to search far and wide for understanding. It's thanks to my identities as a psychoanalytic candidate and psychiatrist that I was introduced to these pages, but it's because of all the other identities I hold that I felt them deeply—woman, mother, immigrant. This volume is for anyone interested in understanding the complex psychological factors that characterize the often missing, misunderstood, or misrepresented experience of women. While covering a range of psychoanalytic concepts and history, the book is current, ardent, and inherently a conversation.

545

They say one should never judge a book by its cover. Avgi Saketopoulou's Sexuality beyond Consent is the rare exception. The cover art was created by Jee-Young Lee, a South Korean conceptual artist known for constructing surrealistic images staged in her single-room art studio. In this particular piece the artist sits on the ground, with her back to the viewer, cracking absurdly large eggs over boulder-size rocks. It is the perfect visual metaphor for this book. Saketopoulou lets us know from the outset that she too intends to break a few eggs as she challenges us to go beyond what we think we know about human sexuality, trauma, and race.

When I was about nine, I wrote a story called “The Winged Boat.” In it a boy builds a little boat out of wood and, with a twist of magic (or imagination) that I now don't recall, gives it the power of flying. He then loads it with what he thinks will be needed to sail across the ocean to Angola, where, he presumes, people will understand his Portuguese. I also illustrated it, with simple but effective (I believe) drawings. I put the story into an envelope and mailed it to what then was the most prominent publishing house in Brazil. Owen Lewis

Owen Lewis

11 East 87th Street
New York, NY 10128

Owen Lewis   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Department of Medical Humanities and Ethics, Vagelos College of Physicians and Surgeons, Columbia University.

As if anticipating the question “Who should write a memoir?” Robert Pinsky devotes a chapter (titled “Magic Mountain”) to a consideration of 559 fame. He mentions a conversation on a plane with a passenger who remarks, seeing him reading a book by Robert Lowell, recently deceased, “He seems like someone who should be famous.” Pinsky then describes the rescheduling of a meeting with Robert Hass, himself, and Czeslaw Milosz about translations. Between a second and third change of schedule, Milosz had won the Nobel Prize, which changed their communications. Pinsky mentions the fame of Edgar Guest in his day and the relative obscurity of Elizabeth Bishop, and the reversal of that status today.

To me this chapter reveals some anxiety in this important man of letters, a former poet laureate, about how he will be remembered and if he should be writing a memoir at all. It would seem, however, that Pinsky believes that the famous, or at least the accomplished, need no rationale for writing a memoir. And yet the less than famous have often written compelling memoirs. What draws a reader to a memoir that comes without the advertisement of fame? A reader should have some clear expectations of what's in store. Kate Daniels, for instance, is an important American poet but with a reputation more limited than Pinsky's. She recently wrote (and I reviewed in JAPA 70/4) a memoir titled Short Fuse of the Possible: A Memoir in Poetry and Psychoanalysis. Her title sets expectations, which the memoir compellingly delivers—a quote from Emily Dickinson and a focused account of exploration of poetry and during her psychoanalysis.

It's not clear what expectations are set by Pinsky's title, and that, to my mind, undermines the memoir. First, Jersey Breaks—we'll learn something about New Jersey. “Breaks” is a poetically vague word. Has the state given him a break? (An epigraph to the book reads, “Gimme a break”—Folk Saying.) Was everyone there waiting for a break? Was poetry his break? Is there some news about to break? A line break? Or, as the poet must intend, an allusion to a breaking wave. The book is set in a New Jersey shore town, Long Branch, and details his growing up there in the 1940s and 1950s. Moving beyond this poetic title, the substance of the book would seem to be contained in the second part of the title, Becoming an American Poet, which sets a far grander expectation. Thus, I began reading this memoir with this phrase in mind, and looked for its fulfillment.

Owen Lewis   Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, Department of Medical Humanities and Ethics, Vagelos College of Physicians and Surgeons, Columbia University.

It's 1958. I'm seven—reading Scholastic Magazine, intrigued by the page of poems, children's ditties. The words are not aligned in paragraphs but stand alone in shorter lines. The lines sometimes rhyme and have a rhythm different from what I'm used to hearing, though I couldn't then articulate it that way. There are many surprises. I am looking at every word in a new way. Take one word out, the poem crumbles like a game of Jenga. This sounds like the same language in which I'm told to pick up my things—but it's different. Very different. This language has a magic. Equally intriguing, the ages of authors are listed: John Somebody, age eight, Caroline, age eleven. Children like me had written these incantations. If they had, so could I! So I began to play with words as I played with toys, and sometime in the next year I, too, had my first publication. What I take from this memory: magic and permission.

It's 2008 (a few years later). I'm on a train coming back from a child psychiatry meeting in Washington D.C. I was not writing poetry at this point in my life, but in the last month or two had noticed random phrases, fragments, and lines accumulating in my appointment book, in the margins of my patient notes, in my telephone log. I had given little attention to these fragments until that train ride. From the window passing the demarcated farm fields of rural Maryland or New Jersey, I began to jot down similar fragments as I imagined a giant jogging alongside the train, leaping from field to field. The rhythm of the train and the rhythm of the appearance of these fields must have helped the fragments form into lines and the lines into—a poem! After more than twenty years of my not writing poetry or fiction, a poem appeared. I looked at it in recognition of a long lost friend, one on whose face is recognized both the person of youth and the person of today. I felt, at that moment, a keen sense of a calling, a remembered calling. As much as I was “called” to be a physician, a

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Racism, Activity, and Passivity in the Analytic Dyad: A Fanonian Meditation

Elliott Schwebach

Elliott Schwebach

Albuquerque, NM 87131-0001
Email: elliottschwebach@gmail.com

Elliott Schwebach  

Fanon, analytic neutrality, activity, passivity, racism, coloniality, white supremacy

Frantz Fanon's reception within psychoanalysis has been hindered by an interpretive “snag” that vexes discussions of his work and relevance. This “snag” misleadingly situates Fanon's clinical approach as necessarily outside, or antithetical to, treatment as conceived and practiced in the Freudian tradition. As a result, analytic educators, students, and therapists are prone to position Fanon on one side of a conceptual boundary and “analytic neutrality” on the other. This reading is not only misguided but detrimental to the healing potential and continued development of psychoanalysis. A closer look at one of Fanon's oft-repeated rallying cries, in which its context is examined and its intent unpacked, allows for a disambiguating of “analytic neutrality” and affords a number of takeaways that can help readers recognize the stakes of Fanon's contributions to psychoanalysis and appreciate their pertinence for dyadic clinical treatment. A major implication is the importance for psychoanalysis, in both pedagogy and clinical practice, to take coloniality (the continued legacies of colonial domination, including especially white supremacy) far more seriously.

Mark Goldblatt  

Introduction: David Lichtenstein

The title given to this discussion—a panel presented in June 2022—is something of a provocation: Thanatos: Is Freud's Concept Still Relevant? In fact, the human capacity for violent destruction has never been more evident. War, senseless murder, the despoiling of nature, the exploitation and degradation of human life—do we psychoanalysts have something relevant to say about this? Can we sort out our different ideas about the sources and meanings of this capacity for death, killing, and destruction?

Thanatos is a somewhat metaphysical concept that Freud introduced in the development of his idea of Todestrieb. I use the German word advisedly because its translation as either death drive or death instinct immediately raises questions about Freud's intended meaning. So simply unpacking the title of this discussion—Thanatos / death drive / death instinct—suggests lines of difference and debate about what Freud was up to and what use we analysts might now make of it. It is our intention

Rachel Widawsky  

In our opening essay, Peter Rudnystky takes Austin Ratner's The Psychoanalyst's Aversion to Proof as the launching site for a lively debate regarding the historical and theoretical underpinnings of psychoanalytic “proof aversion.” Rudnytsky admires Ratner's courage and persistence, and the depth of his thinking. “Who can fail to admire an independent scholar,” Rudnytsky asks, “who, in 2012, singlehandedly waged a battle ‘to edit the lead paragraph of Freud's Wikipedia page,’ even though he knew that every morning he ‘would face a torrent of irrational hostility’ from an opponent?” Rudnytsky differs with Ratner's view that “Freud's proof aversion [is] the root cause of his problems, and his authoritarianism … an effect of that underlying tendency.” Rudnystky argues that “where Ratner falls short is in his perfunctory references to hermeneutics, which do not take into account that, both as an encounter between the unique subjectivities of two human beings in the clinical setting and as a method of investigation into every facet of culture, the hermeneutic dimension of psychoanalysis is ineluctable.” In Rudnystky's view psychoanalysis is, ultimately, “as a theory of mind … a natural science, but as both a clinical practice and a method of critical inquiry it is a historical science; and it is a category error to suppose that interpretations can ever be demonstrated with mathematical certainty.”

Mourning and the Body

In Mourning: Its Traces in Character and the Body (Los duelos: Huellas en el carácter y registro corpora), Marillé Truscello de Manson, an Argentinian psychoanalyst renowned for her publications on mourning, brings to this concept a novel approach, complementary to the traditional object-relations perspective. In her review, Marie-Ange Pongis relates Truscello's metapsychological and clinical approach to mourning in the context of early somatic pathologies. She argues that “what is not psychically elaborated leaves traces in the body,” and produces somatic 705

Los Duelos: Huellas en el Carácter y Registro Corpora (Mourning: Its Traces in Character and the Body). By Marilé Truscello de Manson. Buenos Aires: Biebel, 2013, 312 pp.

Marie Pongis

Marie Pongis

1180 Avenue Murray
Québec, Qc G1S3B6
CANADA
Email: marieangepongis@logeweb.com

Marie Pongis   Member, Quebec Psychoanalytic Society.

Marilé Truscello de Manson, an Argentinian psychoanalyst, has long been interested in the consequences of mourning on the psyche and the body. Since the 1991 publication of her doctoral thesis, The Process of Mourning and Changes in Character, she has continued to fine-tune her clinical observations and deepen her theoretical reflections on the notion that what is not psychically elaborated leaves traces in the body. The significance of mourning and early fixations, combined with somatic pathologies, are the three principal axes of her book Mourning: Its Traces in Character and the Body.

The author highlights two areas: on one hand she explores the psychic processes at play in character formation and the transmission of trauma, while on the other she investigates the coexistence of addictions or psychosomatic pathologies. Such expressions of trauma lead her to include the problem of bodily disturbances or changes in the body as they pertain to character disorders. The latter pose a difficult challenge during the treatment of patients who do not take care of themselves but create difficulties by focusing on the external world.

Studies of character usually order the appearance of character traits or problems with identifications. In this book, however, de Manson proposes a new approach: she considers how the traumatic situation brings about changes in character. She highlights the economic factor and its role in the production of character traits embedded in pathological mourning.

In chapter 1, “The Ego and Defense Mechanisms in Character,” based on both Freudian and post-Freudian theory, de Manson explores the link between infantile trauma and ego defenses against drives and 727

Nonlinear Psychoanalysis: Notes from Forty Years of Chaos and Complexity Theory. By Robert M. Galatzer-Levy. New York: Routledge, 2017, xii + 272 pp., $190.00 hardcover, $64.95 paperback.

Rebecca Chaplan

Rebecca Chaplan

40 West 77th Street, 1F
New York, NY 10024
Email: rlchaplan@gmail.com

Rebecca Chaplan   Training and Supervising Analyst, Columbia University Center for Psychoanalytic Training and Research; Assistant Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, New York Presbyterian Hospital.

This is a long overdue review of a well-written and informative book that deserves to be widely read and reread. Robert Galatzer-Levy has compiled in one volume several papers containing his contributions over the past forty years to a new conceptualization of psychoanalytic theory, consistent with theoretical advances in mathematics that were developed to study complex systems. A clear and engaging teacher, he is uniquely positioned to guide us through this territory. He not only explains the meanings of esoteric terms—attractors, emergent properties, coupled oscillators, fractals—but also shows how these concepts are relevant and appropriate to the study of interactions in the complex dynamic system of an analytic encounter, and how they address long-standing dilemmas in classical theory.

The case examples, though, are where the book will have the most value to the practicing analyst, with well-chosen vignettes that illustrate what this perspective adds for thinking about our work. This book serves as a thorough and intelligible introduction to concepts that have entered our vocabulary and will inevitably be important in any coherent theory of analytic process. Though rigorous, it is never dry. Packed with information it never strays far from the question of what all of this has to do with caring for patients. It is also a memoir of a life immersed in teaching and learning. In fact, the best way I can describe Galatzer-Levy's book is as a love letter to the world of ideas. His enthusiasm is contagious, and I suspect that anyone who reads it will come away educated and renewed.

Maybe You Should Talk to Someone: A Therapist, Her Therapist, and Our Lives Revealed. By Lori Gottlieb. Boston: Houghton Mifflin Harcourt, 2019, xii + 420 pp., $28.00 hardcover, $19.99 paperback.

Elliot Jurist

Elliot Jurist

680 West End Avenue
New York, NY 10025

Elliot Jurist   Professor of Psychology and Philosophy, City College of New York and the Graduate Center, City University of New York.

Lori Gottlieb's cheeky title, Maybe You Should Talk to Someone: A Therapist, Her Therapist, and Our Lives Revealed, produced the 737 anticipatory alarm that the book that might best fit the self-help genre. The more I became immersed in this 400-plus-page book, though, the more I came to appreciate Gottlieb's determination to craft a work that celebrates long-term psychotherapy without unduly simplifying psychoanalytic constructs and values. No one can fail to be impressed that the book has sold over a million copies since its publication in 2019 (when it appeared on several best-book-of-the-year lists). Since then an accompanying workbook has been published, in 2021, and a TV series is being planned. The book's success, I suspect, is due to its sensitive treatment of the roles of both patient and therapist and its candor in revealing how they impact each other to foster change in the life of the patient.

Gottlieb presents four cases in detail from her private practice, interspersed with her own story, with special attention to her experience in therapy, prompted by a relationship's coming to a disappointing end. Her personal journey is convoluted: we do not learn much about her early life, though we are told she has a close relationship with her father and a conflicted one with her mother.

The story picks up as she describes her first career in Hollywood after graduating from Stanford, how she shifted direction by going to medical school, and then pivoted again to take an unspecified degree in psychology (her Wikipedia page says she has an M.A, from Pepperdine and is licensed in marriage and family therapy). At the center of her story is her own ongoing therapy, which got under way when she reluctantly arranged to meet “Wendell,” having sought a referral from a colleague, allegedly for someone else. Gottlieb mentions that she had been in therapy before, and I would have been curious to hear about that in greater detail. Still, Gottlieb validates a fundamental part of the psychoanalytic tradition in highlighting how her own therapy has been instrumental in her development as a therapist.

Freud (1926) described obsessional neurosis as “the most interesting and repaying subject of analytic research” (p. 112). In spite of the simplicity of his theory of an anal fixation, he did not believe that psychoanalysis had arrived at a satisfactory explanation of its origin or structure. While the advantage of the disorder lay in the obsessive patient's verbal presentation—in contrast with the hysteric's more obscure bodily pains—the difficulty lay in the “variety” of its forms, in the sweeping range, that is, of the problems we regard as obsessive (p. 155).

In a scholarly and wide-ranging contribution, Moshe Marcus and Steven Tuber seek to explain just one of the core features of obsessive mental functioning—doubt. The obsessive subject lacks a feeling of certainty in cases (e.g., repeatedly makes sure the door is locked) where certainty would seem warranted. Although the cognitive and neuropsychological literature has emphasized the problem of failure to achieve what Marcus and Tuber term affective certainty (Reed 1977; Dar et al. 2000; Taillefer et al. 2016), we lack a developmental theory to account for it. The authors seek to provide a theory, one grounded in ego psychological and Winnicottian thinking, along with a description of the mental processes that underlie this capacity.

Hannah Geller

Hannah Geller

310 West 97th Street
New York, NY 10025
Email: hannahgellersw@gmail.com

Hannah Geller   Adult and Child Candidate, Psychoanalytic Association of New York.

Our field is in crisis. The amount of psychoanalysis practiced in the United States has declined dramatically. Institutes can't attract new candidates and candidates can't find new cases. Our reputation has sunk, from idealized to denigrated. And much analysis today occurs over phone or video, which some consider a pale imitation of the in-office experience.

How can we address these problems? Are they even worth solving? The behavior of many analysts indicates a belief that they are not. Most graduate analysts see, on average, one analytic case a week, and many do not practice analysis at all (Kaplan et al. 2009). It seems that we are voting with our feet, and our feet have given up.

In Psychoanalysis from the Inside Out, Lena Ehrlich uses technique and theory that are at once revolutionary and traditional to address our dilemma. She looks not to economics or neuroscience, but rather to our own field, to deepen existential questions about analysis and propose ways of addressing them. Above all, Ehrlich seeks to understand the issues by analyzing them. Though she acknowledges external barriers to analysis—time commitment, expense, unfavorable cultural climate—she redirects our attention toward internal obstructions. [[RunningHead]]

Drafting a Self

Hannah Geller

Hannah Geller

310 West 97th Street
New York, NY 10025
Email: hannahgellersw@gmail.com

Hannah Geller   Adult and Child Candidate, Psychoanalytic Association of New York.

I first realized writing was important to me as an undergraduate, when I decided to major in literature. I had planned to study biology, in line with my family's expectations. But I realized that I felt most alive when I was reading fiction. For me, studying literature was an act of self-assertion. When I turned away from my college's Science Hill and toward the Humanities Center, my own voice—not others' expectations—guided me for the first time.

Central to the literature major was literary theory, which is writing about writing. Literary theory studies how thought, communication, and language form meaning. These inquiries spoke to my young adult preoccupations of identity, abstract thought, and relationship, which would soon lead me to psychoanalysis.

The language theorist Roland Barthes postulated that each piece of writing contains multitudinous meanings, and each act of reading constructs the meaning anew. Writer and reader construct textual meaning in a dynamic and mutual process. Analogously, Roy Schafer conceived of psychoanalysis as making meaning of the self through storytelling. Analyst and analysand together listen to and craft coherence from the events of the analysand's life. In this way, they act as both readers and writers, forming a narrative of the patient's identity. This narrative is in constant flux, as the person acts and interacts in new ways each day. These concepts resonated with me as I considered my own identity. As I learned that the author does not predetermine the text, I was also learning that my family did not predetermine my selfhood. Rather, I had some input into the question of who I was.

The idea that textual meaning transmutes was revolutionary and destabilizing. But in destabilization, Barthes found a special type of pleasure he called jouissance. Detaching the text from its essentialist meaning

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lacan in America: A Special Issue

Sarah Ackerman , Gregory Rizzolo and Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Email: ackerman@myyahoo.com

greg.rizzolo@depthcounseling.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2897-3931

mdwilsonmd@berkeley.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-8681-136X

Mitchell Wilson  

In January 1964, soon after the International Psychoanalytical Association had marginalized him, Jacques Lacan opened his seminar at the École Normale Supérieure in Paris with a question: Was he qualified to speak on the subject of psychoanalysis? A “deal” had been struck, he told his audience (1964, p. 4); members of the Société Française de Psychoanalyse (SFP) had successfully gained admission to the IPA in exchange for his being removed from their roster of training analysts. “My teaching,” Lacan explained, “has been the object of censure,” what amounted to a “major excommunication” (p. 3). A great divide was thereby established in the psychoanalytic landscape, one that has had enduring consequences for psychoanalysis in the decades that followed up to the present time.1 Though splits are not uncommon in the history of psychoanalysis, in Lacan's case a person and his “teaching”—his psychoanalytic work both clinical and theoretical—were forever congealed into one and the same thing.2

With regard to psychoanalytic education and training in the United States, these efforts at marginalization were largely successful. Even today, when American training programs typically include a course on each major school of thought, and when psychoanalytic journals ask authors to engage with an ever widening range of perspectives, many American psychoanalysts know little about the Lacanian orientation. Notably, while American clinicians have largely ignored or simply don't 1Though many of Lacan's seminars have been translated into English, and there is a substantial post-Lacanian literature on a variety of psychoanalytic topics, almost none of this work can be found on PEP-Web. This fact is a telling instance of the divide we are describing here, one that has had enormous practical effects regarding the dissemination, or in this case the impediment, of a significant vein of psychoanalytic thought.2This identity equation, namely that “Lacan” and his theory-building are one and the same thing, indissoluble, often leads, on the one hand, to default appeals to authority by Lacanian analysts (“Lacan said …”) and, on the other, to ad hominem attacks on the person Lacan as a way to discount or ignore the theory, both its merits and its limitations (for a recent example of the latter, see Diatkine 2023).

responseSet.title: Lacan: A Second Chance in America, November 1975
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lacan: A Second Chance in America, November 1975

Sherry Turkle

Sherry Turkle

77 Massachusetts Avenue, Room E51-185B
Cambridge, MA 02139
Email: sturkle@media.mit.edu

Sherry Turkle  

It was fall 1975 and Jacques Lacan was coming to America. He wanted to give seminars at Columbia, Yale, Harvard, and MIT. I was to be his point person in Cambridge, not because I was famous or well-connected (I was twenty-seven and still writing my dissertation on the history of French psychoanalysis), but I was available and in his debt.

For several years before this visit, I had been in France, studying what I termed the French “infatuation” with Freud. I framed my work as an intellectual history mystery. Why did a country that didn't even have a standard edition of Freud in the early 1960s come to have a vibrant psychoanalytic culture by the mid-1970s? I interviewed analysts and analysts in training. And I also interviewed a collection of philosophers, doctors, journalists, political activists, social workers, teachers, and psychologists taken up by the psychoanalytic enthusiasm that was sweeping the professions. In France, massage therapists and high school students wanted to be in psychoanalysis. In the United States, classical analysis was considered passé. In France, psychoanalysts were in demand—but only a linguistic, Cartesian brand of psychoanalysis embodied in the person and ideas of seventh arrondissement Jacques Lacan, head of L'École Freudienne de Paris.

But for me, there was more to Lacan's popularity than the idea that the French had found a Catholic and French Freud. My working hypothesis: In the aftermath of the failed student uprising of May 1968, Lacan's notions about the centrality of what he called the symbolic order became a way to think through the political ideas of May. May ideology insisted that there was no line between the political and the personal. Lacan

Abby Rockefeller Mauzé Professor of the Social Sciences of Science and Technology, Massachusetts Institute of Technology.

Invited paper, August 24, 2023.

responseSet.title: America's Lacan Après Coup
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

America's Lacan Après Coup

Humphrey Morris

Humphrey Morris

8 Irving Terrace
Cambridge, MA 02138
Email: humphreymorris@gmail.com

Humphrey Morris  

acknowledgment, disavowal, feminism, Freudian theory, history

Lacan's effect in America was dramatic but limited following his 1975 visit. His polemic with ego psychology in Écrits radically changed the way literary critics, notably feminist critics, thought about psychoanalysis, while in those same years—the 1970s and 1980s—American psychoanalysts, taken up with their own reactions to ego psychology, paid him little attention. Yet après coup, looking back at that period, Lacan can be counted among those who contributed importantly to a major shift in our conception of psychoanalytic process: our contemporary sense of acts of reading—including clinical listening—as acts in themselves, rather than as steps toward the interpretive determination of hidden meaning. In acts of reading inspired by Lacan, feminist critics helped free Freud's theory of disavowal from its origins in the male anxieties of the castration complex. Speaking as the disavowed “others” of psychoanalysis, Lacan's feminist readers also went beyond him in moving psychoanalysis toward acknowledgment of questions of social and historical reality, including its own. Regarding this evolution, it can be speculated that hidden behind the bitterness of the split in the 1950s and 1960s between Lacan and the once European, now American ego psychologists can be found an unconscious agreement. On both sides of the Atlantic, psychoanalysis had had its reasons, if different reasons, to disavow for years the ways it was implicated in the unspeakable trauma of recent European history.

responseSet.title: The Reception of Lacanian Theory and Practice by American Psychoanalytic Training Programs
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Reception of Lacanian Theory and Practice by American Psychoanalytic Training Programs

Lewis Kirshner

Lewis Kirshner

3517 Duck Pond Road
Waterford, VT 05819
Email: lewiskirshner@gmail.com

Lewis Kirshner  

Lacan, psychoanalysis, IPA, Freud, Widlöcher, J, -A, Miller, psychoanalytic technique

This paper explores the principal reasons for the exclusion of Lacanian ideas from psychoanalytic training institutes in the United States. The history of Lacan's role in the International Psychoanalytical Association, from which essentially he was expelled, occupies a central place in this story. Significant issues arose also from his practice style and technical innovations, whose rationale remains controversial today. Another major obstacle for the reception of his work is the theoretical framework of Lacanian analysis, so different from that of other schools. Inclusion of its unfamiliar vocabulary and concepts poses practical problems for training programs. At a more fundamental level, the strong antihumanist evolution of Lacan's thought runs contrary to the increasingly relational and intersubjective orientation of American psychoanalysis. The incompatibility between the disparate languages of a scientific theory aiming at objectivity and a phenomenology of personal intentionality and meaning greatly limits the possibilities for dialogue. The tension between these perspectives cannot be resolved, but a productive exchange between them is possible if they are accepted as valid and complementary ways of speaking about human behavior.

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: Lucia Tower's “Countertransference”

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Email: mdwilsonmd@berkeley.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-8681-136X

Mitchell Wilson  

Lucia Tower's sole publication1 is the paper reprinted here titled “Countertransference,” first published in JAPA in 1956. “Countertransference” stands on its own as a psychoanalytic paper of enduring value—direct and unguarded, gutsy and thoughtful—written at the zenith of ego psychology's hegemony in the United States. One need only consider the impact of Kurt Eissler's “The Effect of the Structure of the Ego on Psychoanalytic Technique” (1953) to see how Tower's intervention cut across the grain of American psychoanalysis at that time, as she forged her own path.

Eissler's paper, so influential in American psychoanalysis in the 1950s, falls within the genre of “ideal theory.”2 He says as much in the paper's opening section:

Psychoanalytic techniques depend chiefly on three variables: the patient's disorder and personality, the patient's present life circumstances, and the psychoanalyst's personality. In the following discussion the last two variables will be excluded. It is assumed that the living conditions of the patient and the personality of the analyst are both ideal; that is, entirely favorable to the analytic process. Thus in our assumption no disturbance of the psychoanalytic process originates either from the patient's actual life circumstances or the analyst's personality [1953, p. 105].

From this starting position, Eissler unfolds his well-known “parameters” argument; he defines a parameter as “the deviation, both quantitative and qualitative, from the basic model technique, that is to say, from a 1Tower's only other publication was a three-page summary, written with Victor Calef, of a roundtable discussion of the training analyst system at a conference of the American Psychoanalytic Association (Tower and Calef 1954).2Ideal theory is a branch of political philosophy. Such efforts “do not offer solutions to real world problems; instead the aim of ideal theory is to provide a guide for improvements based on what society should normatively appear to be” (from the Wikipedia article “Ideal Theory (Politics).

responseSet.title: Countertransference
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Countertransference

Lucia Tower

Lucia Tower

Lucia Tower  

Theoretical Considerations

References to countertransference appeared very early in psychoanalytic literature. Originally, they paid mostly lip service to its existence, with unelaborated statements that, of course, analysts could have transference reactions to their patients. Little else was said, other than to imply that these were dubious reactions and should be controlled, and for analysts to discuss their counter-transference reactions in public would be somewhat indecently self-revealing. About ten years ago, a moderate number of articles began to appear. The general overtone of these articles has been of a rather embarrassed sort, as though these were major imperfections in our therapeutic procedures, and of course certain countertransference phenomena are considered reprehensible in the extreme.

The literature on countertransference has recently been excellently reviewed by Douglass Orr (1954). I shall make only cursory comments about this literature because my main purpose is to present some ideas of my own and some detailed case material. Despite wide agreement among analysts about transference, there has been wide disagreement about countertransference. Freud's first reference to it in 1910 was rather forbidding: “We have begun to consider the ‘counter-transference’ … arising as a result of the patient's influence on his [the physician's] unconscious feelings, and have nearly come to the point of requiring the physician to recognize and overcome this counter-transference in himself ” (1910, p. 289).

Read before the Chicago Psychoanalytic Society, May 1955, and the American Psychoanalytic Association in New York, December 1955.

This article was originally published as: Tower, L.E. (1956). Countertransference.Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association4:224-255. The original is doi: 10.1177/000306515600400202.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Anxiety, Desire, and the Object A: Lacan on Lucia Tower's “Countertransference”

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Email: mdwilsonmd@berkeley.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-8681-136X

Mitchell Wilson  

Jacques Lacan, Lucia Tower, anxiety, desire, object a, countertransference, sadism, seminar, reading

Lacan's seminars are a treasure trove of innovative psychoanalytic explorations. In Seminar X, Anxiety, he takes up this Freudian theme and explores a number of interrelated ideas: castration, the difference between the sexes, two different forms of acting out, and what he terms his only original theoretical contribution: the object a and its “various incidences.” The object a is described here in detail, especially in relation to Lacan's argument that analysts who are women have a freer relationship to their desire and the countertransferences it spawns than do men. Lacan discussed Lucia Tower's classic paper, “Countertransference,” in light of these notions. This essay is a close reading of Lacan's close reading of Tower, whose account, he says, must be approached in all its “innocence and freshness.”

[[RunningHead]]

From the Editors

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3372-3750

jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-6104-8978

rsboue@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-2403-0549

Rachel Widawsky  

Perspectives on Lacan

Any scholar—whether studying timeless sacred texts, ancient Greek mythology, Joyce's Ulysses, or the works of William Shakespeare—knows the sensation of drilling deeper and deeper, each turn revealing a new meaning or a new understanding of an entire body of work. The range and depth of interpretations offered by devoted scholars—those who plumb texts for newly discovered or freshly-linked meanings—provide a deepened appreciation for the works at hand. In this issue, we offer four very different reviews pertaining to the work of Lacan. Each reviewer brings new insights and innovative approaches to diverse aspects of Lacanian thought. Each seeks to interpret and bring meaning to the complexity of Lacan's written words, while commenting thoughtfully on the contributions of authors and editors who have studied and compiled these works. Our reviewers' observations span a range of topics that highlight why scholars continue to return to the works of Lacan—brilliant teacher, complex thinker, often inscrutable writer.

God, it has often been said, is in the details. In his book essay, “The Need for Nothing: Lacanian Theory and the Sacred,” Todd Dean offers a far-reaching exploration on Lacan's ideas about religion and the sacred, exploring from within a Lacanian framework the notion of mystery, the sacred, and the unknowable. Dean reflects that from Lacan's point of view, “The point is, there is an unknown—and unknowable—Thing that the subject is always striving to know and engage.” Examining the writings of Richard Boothby (Embracing the Void: Rethinking the Origin of the Sacred), Carl Waitz and Theresa Clement Tisdale (Lacanian Psychoanalysis and Eastern Orthodox Christian Anthropology in Dialogue), and Rob Weatherill (Lacan in the End Times), Dean considers the psychological underpinnings of religious philosophies and guides the reader through Lacan's views on religion and psychoanalysis.

Rodrigo Barahona   Member, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute and Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis.

The unconscious, of principal interest for psychoanalysis, is atemporal. But is it also stateless?

—Silvia Heimburger

Books on psychoanalysis in Latin America have the daunting task of describing to their readers how a global profession, theory, and practice as fluid and dynamic as psychoanalysis assumes a particular shape within the physical and cultural geographies of an equally liquid part of the world. Others have come before this massive volume (see, e.g., Litman 1966; Lemlij 1993; Lewkowitz and Flechner 2005), but none with the same breadth of theory, creativity, and originality as Fernando Martín Gómez and Jean Marc Tauszik's Contemporary Latin American Psychoanalysis: Volume One. This book is essential to the library of any psychoanalytic institute whose members and candidates are interested in immersing themselves in the vital pulse and deep history of this Latin American psychoanalytic moment.

It is both a pleasure and a challenge to review a work of this magnitude. This first volume alone spans 1,388 pages and contains seven sections, each housing anywhere from nine to eleven papers. The informative and finely written two-part introduction by the editors frames a rich array of selected work from some of the region's finest minds. In doing so, they present a slice of the Latin American intellectual psychoanalytic milieu and reveal the interwoven layers that sediment what can 989

In the preface to Embracing the Void: Rethinking the Origin of the Sacred, Richard Boothby is clear that Freud approached the topic of religion with “unrelenting hostility” (p. 1). At the same time, Boothby writes that “the religious impulse is uniquely illuminated by the lessons of psychoanalysis,” while acknowledging that psychoanalysis is itself facing unrelenting hostility from many parts of the clinical and academic worlds. Boothby's introduction focuses on what cannot be known by the human subject, an issue he sees as fundamental to Lacanian theory, as well as to religious experience. As Boothby notes, Freud's emphasis, in oedipal theory, on the infant's desire for the mother and resentment of the father assumes that the subject has some grasp of what matters to him. What Lacan emphasizes, in contrast, is the mystery of the desire of the mother, that is, what the infant does not and can never understand regarding both what the mother wants and what he makes of his own desire for her. “In every entry into language, in every iteration of signifying material,” Boothby writes, “there resounds some echo of the unanswered and unanswerable question of the Other” (p. 58). I highly recommend Reading Lacan's Écrits: From ‘Signification of the Phallus’ to ‘Metaphor of the Subject‘—the third of three volumes studying each text entry of the Écrits, edited by Stijn Vanheule, Derek Hook, and Calum Neill—to readers already familiar to some degree with Lacan's work. Each of the chapters, written by invited authors, examines one of the last eleven entries in the Écrits. Each author presents contextual material situating the entry within Lacan's work, followed by commentary formatted as a high-power microscope: a paragraph-by-paragraph, even line-by-line examination of the text. The series is not, in my opinion, a good choice for readers seeking an introduction to the Écrits, much less an imaginary “right answer” to the question of what the Lacanian model “is” and what each entry in the Écrits is “about.”

A back-cover blurb by Slavoj Žižek states, “Let's face it: Lacan's Écrits, one of the classic texts of modern thought, are unreadable—they remain so if we just pick the thick volume up and start to read it.” The editors take these difficulties as the main topic of their introduction, stating in their second sentence that the Écrits are “baroque, intimidating, [and] ever elusive. … Rather than the Rosetta Stone that enables the unlocking of other obscure writings, Lacan's Écrits is far more akin to a literary Babel. … [It] is not a book; it is a type of infinite text; it does not end, it cannot be finished; it continues to escape the ‘imaginarization’ of our attempts at assimilation” (pp. xvii, xx). The editors thereby express every reader's and writer's response when attempting to read or write about Lacan, and their introductory warning has been given before in earlier studies of the Écrits. For example, this volume contains commentary by Fabio Vighi on “The Subversion of the Subject and the Dialectic 1025

Dany Nobus

Dany Nobus

Kingston Lane
Uxbridge UB8 3PH
United Kingdom
Email: dany.nobus@brunel.ac.uk

Dany Nobus   Professor of Psychoanalytic Psychology, Brunel University London; Founding Scholar, British Psychoanalytic Council; former Chair and Fellow, the Freud Museum London.

On paper, writing a monograph or putting together an anthology dealing with Lacan's views on depression and melancholia comes across as a futile undertaking. It is like conceiving a book on Freud's opinions about alcoholism or dipsomania. In both cases, the clinical labels were very much part of the standard psychiatric tool kit when the authors developed 1029 their ideas, yet neither author paid much attention to them (Madden 1995; Berrios 1988; 1996, pp. 289-331). The lack of a solid psychoanalytic theory of alcoholism in Freud's work can be attributed plausibly, yet nonetheless hypothetically, to the observation that in his private practice he seldom got to see patients who suffered from chronic alcohol abuse. In Lacan's case, the glaring absence of a thorough theoretical explanation and concurrent clinical psychoanalytic approach for depression and melancholia is more puzzling. For many years Lacan worked both as a psychiatrist and as a psychoanalyst, which would have significantly broadened his exposure to a variety of clinical conditions. Also, Lacan's infamous habit of conducting short sessions implies that he most likely saw more patients during his lifetime than any other psychoanalyst in history, some of whom must have come with a diagnosis or symptoms of depression or melancholia.

In the introduction to this interesting collection of essays, the editors, Derek Hook and Stijn Vanheule, state that they are not particularly surprised by Lacan's limited interest in depression and melancholia. All in all, they offer four reasons for this. First, they point out that the notion of depression “falls short of the clinical and conceptual rigor required of a properly Freudian concept” (p. 1). Second, they claim that “this diagnostic label [of depression] had not attained [in Lacan's time] the pervasive cultural predominance it has today” (p. 2). Third, they contend that “the notion of depression is considered to be under-defined and too all-encompassing” (p. 6). And, finally, they posit that “depressive phenomena should be seen as symptoms that can occur in any structure [neurosis, psychosis, or perversion]” (p. 7), so that the label is not diagnostically useful.

Adieu Lacan. Directed by Richard Ledes. Good Soup Media, 2021, 1 hour, 32 minutes.

Alexander Kalogerakis

Alexander Kalogerakis

400 West End Avenue, Suite 1DD
New York, NY 10024
Email: adkalogerakis@gmail.com

Alexander Kalogerakis   Faculty, New York Psychoanalytic Society and Institute; Associate Attending Psychiatrist, New York-Presbyterian Hospital; Clinical Associate Professor of Psychiatry, Weill Cornell Medical College.

Adieu Lacan, a film by Richard Ledes, offers much to the psychoanalytic viewer. The movie, a fictionalized version of a treatment conducted by Jacques Lacan in the 1970s, relies on two accounts by Betty Milan, the patient in the treatment. Milan, herself a psychiatrist and Lacanian analyst, wrote the play Goodbye Doctor (2008), and a novel, Lacan's Parrot (1997), both of which use her treatment and later collaboration with Lacan as source material. I will review the movie from my perspective as an analyst trained primarily in the Freudian ego psychology tradition, and offer some thoughts related to technique, pathogenesis, development, and culture.

The movie is set almost entirely in the consulting room. We see an analysis represented by fragments of fourteen sessions over three years. But we are also privy to the thoughts of both main characters. For clarity, I will refer to this cinematic version of Lacan as “the Doctor,” as Milan does in her play.

Soon after “Seriema” begins treatment, she tells the Doctor about her immigrant family and how they were disappointed that she was a girl. This is followed by information about her two miscarriages, which prompted her husband to leave her. After the first miscarriage, she was told she had a medical problem but did not pursue treatment, a decision that led to the second miscarriage. The Doctor is surprised and underlines her choice to not seek treatment. He is starting to interest Seriema in the power of her unconscious. Then he abruptly ends the session.

This is the first of several such endings in the movie. It shows Lacan's technique of the variable length session. According to Bruce Fink (2019), these unexpected endings are intended to highlight something important that has just happened, and to promote the patient's reflection on this new information.

Cutting sessions is part of a larger technical approach in which the analyst aims to frustrate and puzzle the patient, and stimulate the patient's curiosity. Lacan said that good analytic interpretation, especially early in treatment, is like the Delphic oracle's utterances: intriguing but unclear (Fink 1997).

Writing and Intimate Privacy

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

Gower Street
London WC1E 6BT
United Kingdom
Email: dr.alessandra.lemma@gmail.com

Alessandra Lemma  

Writing has never felt like a choice. More of a necessity, like breathing. I published my first paper when I was twenty-five. I have continued to write ever since. Writing and publishing often go hand in hand but not necessarily—at least not for me. First and foremost, I write to discover what I think. I have seldom managed to clarify what I really think about all manner of topics without a pen, paper, and an imagined interlocutor. There is always someone in my mind to whom or for whom I am writing, even if I will never publish it. It is a comforting form of being alone in the presence of a listening internal other. As I write the words, I imagine the agreement as well as disagreement with what I am articulating, and this provides the impetus I need to sharpen my thoughts.

I emphasize the distinction between “writing to discover what I think” and “writing to publish” because I experience them as distinct activities, propelled by different states of mind in relation to both internal and external objects. Writing with the intention of being published requires me to own the narcissistic currents that drive the wish to be published. The desire to share our ideas, to further our field, and to be recognized for our efforts are ambitions, in part at least, that reflect self-interest. Even so, they are legitimate ambitions and, I would add, essential to advancing our development as analysts through exposing ourselves to criticism and challenge.

Writing for publication is an intervention. As a writer I hope to impact my reader. In my experience, the decision to publish inevitably also impacts my relationship with the individual patient whose material my writing may be based on. I have found this to be a very thorny, taxing question that requires me to grapple with the ethics of publication. Until a few years ago, I subscribed to the dominant view that very careful anonymization of clinical material sufficed and that seeking consent was not an ethical requirement. My views on this matter have evolved. I want to use the

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction: W(h)ither Interpretation?

Mitchell Wilson

Mitchell Wilson

Email: mdwilsonmd@berkeley.edu
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-8681-136X

Mitchell Wilson  

The title of this special issue of JAPA is intended to focus the reader on a conundrum within contemporary psychoanalytic clinical theory. First, whither interpretation? The question suggests a futural orientation: Where are we heading? What is to become of psychoanalytic interpretation and its status as we continue to refine our views of the therapeutic process? Second, these questions become especially urgent as a reading of the contemporary literature suggests a moving away—a withering—of interpretation from having a central place in analytic work. Withering in this case means a drying up, a shriveling, a falling into decay. In some analysts' hands the verbal tends to be discounted in deference to the so-called nonverbal, the realm “beyond words.” The dynamics and dimensionality of intersubjective dialogue—what Litowitz (2007) calls the “second person” perspective—take a backseat to a variety of putative processes that fall under Freud's metaphor of the telephone and unconscious communication.1

Is interpretation diminishing—withering? And if not, where—whither—do we place it among the elements that constitute a psychoanalysis?

In considering these questions, it is natural to begin with James Strachey's canonical paper, “The Nature of Therapeutic Action in Psycho-Analysis” (1934). As you will see as you read the offerings in this issue of JAPA, several of our contributors mention, if not begin with, Strachey. As with many papers of enduring value, Strachey's can be read in a number of ways. His focus is on what he calls the “mutative interpretation,” in which the pursuit of truth (i.e., the patient's psychic reality) is embedded in an ongoing, changing transference relationship in the here and now. Strachey's central claim is that a mutative interpretation—not at all a 1Freud (1912) instructed the analyst to “turn his own unconscious, like a receptive organ, towards the transmitting unconscious of the patient. He must adjust himself to the patient as a telephone receiver is adjusted to the transmitting microphone” (pp. 114-115).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Interpretation as Hypothesis

M. Davids

M. Davids

112a Shirland Road
London W9 2BT
UNITED KINGDOM
Email: fakhrydavids@gmail.com

M. Davids  

interpretation, hypothesis, evenly suspended attention, free association, transference, countertransference, depression, mourning, internal racism, scientific method

Two distinct spaces can be seen as operating in a session—a private one in the analyst's mind, where formulations take shape, and one shared between patient and analyst, in which interpretations are offered. By maintaining a focus on the here and now in the latter space, taking care to protect it from intrusions from the analyst's theory except as hypotheses (in the form of interpretations derived from those formulations) aimed at eliciting unconscious responses that further the analytic inquiry, a basis for analytic work is established that aligns with ordinary scientific processes: theory is generated in the mind of the researcher, and hypotheses derived from it are tested systematically in a laboratory setting. Self-understanding that develops out of such an arrangement can then be seen as based on evidence, minimizing the role of suggestion. This line of thinking is illustrated with excerpts from the beginning of the analysis of a depressed patient. In developing areas of theory, when reliable evidence is particularly important, this way of working holds promise. In this case evidence was systematically gathered that led to the formulation of a model of internal racism.

responseSet.title: The Reflexive Function of Psychoanalytic Interpretation
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Reflexive Function of Psychoanalytic Interpretation

David Lichtenstein

David Lichtenstein

80 University Place, 5A
New York, NY 10003
Email: dlichtenstein@gmail.com

David Lichtenstein  

interpretation, discourse, fundamental rule, analytic listening, translation

The act of interpretation in psychoanalysis has a distinct character due to the discursive structure of the psychoanalytic setting. The discourse that issues from the interplay of the fundamental rule and evenly suspended attention is a reflection on reflection. The result is that interpretation instead of being a device for inquiry is itself the object of inquiry. Psychoanalysis does not use interpretation. It is about interpretation itself. This perspective sheds a certain light on longstanding questions about the form and effects of psychoanalytic interpretation.

responseSet.title: Interpreting Interpretation
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Interpreting Interpretation

Morris Eagle

Morris Eagle

4351 Redwood Avenue, #1
Marina del Rey, CA 90292
Email: meagle100@aol.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0002-9332-5714

Morris Eagle  

interpretation, Freudian theory, internalization

Interpretation of the latent meaning of manifest content is the core of the traditional approach to psychoanalytic treatment. The main purpose of such interpretation is to enhance the patient's self-knowledge, in particular his or her awareness of unconscious wishes and their embeddedness in inner conflicts. An assumption of classical psychoanalysis is that veridical interpretations—as Freud put it, interpretations that tally with what is real in the patient—will be especially effective therapeutically. These basic assumptions have been called into question, as reflected in such concepts as “narrative truth” and the overriding importance of the patient's “assured conviction” regarding interpretations. Also called into question is the therapeutic value of “deep” interpretations intended to uncover repressed impulses. To an important extent, these have been replaced by interpretations of defensive processes just below the surface of consciousness, and interpretations that make connections among different experiences, both of which are intended to help the patient understand how his or her mind works. There is also an increased emphasis on nonsemantic aspects of interpretation, as well as some degree of skepticism toward the therapeutic value of interpretation itself, along with an increased emphasis on the implicit interpretive aspects of the therapeutic relationship. Finally, representative research is presented on the relation between transference interpretation and therapeutic outcome.

responseSet.title: What is Meant By the Term Interpretation, and What is it For?
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What is Meant By the Term Interpretation, and What is it For?

Bruce Reis

Bruce Reis

270 Lafayette Street, Suite 807
New York, NY 10012
Email: bruce.reis@nyu.edu

Bruce Reis  

interpretation, noninterpretive

Analysts seem spellbound by language when it comes to the word interpretation, a word so idealized and grand, so laden with fantasy, that the term itself continues to hold a magical sense that defies us to think about it. Called upon to do far too much explanatory work in psychoanalysis, it is accorded a variety of meanings. It is employed for varied uses, often simultaneously, making it hard to know what analysts mean when referring to “interpretation” and what uses they intend for it. Approaches to interpretation may be heuristically separated into those having to do with the content of the unconscious and those attempting to “use” interpretation in a manner still not divorced from content. What it means to attempt to interpret what is by definition uninterpretable is yet another area to be explored. Accordingly, issues of construction, co-construction, and transformation are examined. Every interpretation is at once a concealment, every inscription a negation, every representation a representation of something unrepresentable. Despite this, the concept of interpretation, even if theoretically unsupportable in large measure, retains its clinical utility.

From the Editors

Anne Adelman , Jennifer Stuart and Rachel Widawsky

Rachel Widawsky

Email: anne.adelmanphd@gmail.com
https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3372-3750

jennifer.stuart.phd@gmail.com

rsboue@gmail.com

Rachel Widawsky  

Book reviews engage the work of interpretation and translation. Every review we publish presents an interpretation of an author's work—an attempt to invite readers into conversation with the author and the reviewer about ideas seen through the reviewer's translational lens. In this issue of JAPA devoted to interpretation, we are pleased to inaugurate a unique series of reviews, each corresponding to an entry in the recently launched International Regional Encyclopedic Dictionary (IRED), edited by the IPA and chaired by Eva Papiasvili. In this time of global strife, the IRED project, an online, interregional encyclopedic dictionary of psychoanalysis, speaks to the power of translation and interpretation across a range of perspectives and diversities. IRED is not a dictionary of concepts; rather, it is a dictionary of interpretations of concepts in different regions, languages, and cultures at different times. “The history of our profession starts in a center (Vienna, London, Paris). When it moves toward the periphery, new phenomena occur, and more so when it crosses the oceans. There the fortunate expansion of psychoanalysis intertwines with a variety of factors” (IRED, “Intersubjectivity,” p. 241). One of the key principles of this evolving dictionary is that concepts are not fixed; their meanings migrate across time, space, and languages. In an intricately woven tapestry of interpretation and translation, IRED's editors strive to capture core psychoanalytic concepts in four-dimensional context.

IRED's multiperspectival approach highlights the transformative activity of language in reflecting differences among interpretations, thus building “translational bridges” (Papiasvili 2023). Thanks to the extraordinary collective effort of committed contributors worldwide, IRED embraces plurality without erasing differences. Against our common wish for simplification, IRED embraces a plurality of interpretations, preserving complexity and singularity in our understanding of analytic concepts. IRED reflects an unavoidable tension between words and meanings, which echoes the work of interpretation in psychoanalytic process. In this 1241

Cesare Pavese, the premier twentieth-century poet from Turin, a place of multicultural influences, encouraged the first Italian publications of Freud's work. In his poem “Ancestors” (Pavese 1998) his reflections on coming of age might aptly describe one liberatory arc of analytic aspiration:

Stunned by the world, I reached an age

when I threw punches at air and cried to myself.

Listening to the speech of women and men,

not knowing how to respond, it's not fun.

But this too has passed: I'm not alone anymore,

and if I still don't know how to respond,

I don't need to. Finding myself, I found company.

The Inter-Regional Encyclopedic Dictionary (IRED) is the inspiration of the Italian psychoanalyst Stefano Bolognini and was spawned and cultivated during his tenure as IPA president. Like his compatriot Pavese, Bolognini knows the cacophony of those speaking in different—sometimes discordant—tongues, as well as the emancipatory potential of immersion in a mélange of cultural and linguistic traditions. “We know that different countries and different languages can create … unexpected and new evolutions in the psychoanalytic field that are enriching and can be at the same time shocking for the different communities.”
